rows.concept: When no external force acts on a system (or net external force is zero), total linear momentum is conserved: ΣP_initial = ΣP_final. Follows from Newton's 3rd law applied to internal forces (they cancel pairwise)
rows.notes: MC-G4; **cognitive_error_target:** "momentum conserved always" → only when ΣF_ext = 0

rows.concept: Conservation holds ONLY when net external force is zero. Gravity acting on bouncing ball + earth: SYSTEM is conserved (ball + earth), but ball-alone is NOT. **cognitive_error_target nano**.
rows.notes: parent: conservation_of_momentum_atomic; MC-G4 cognitive_error_target

rows.concept: One body → multiple fragments; total p conserved; KE INCREASES (internal energy → KE). Time-reversed perfectly-inelastic-collision. ISRO multi-stage rocket = controlled explosion at staging events.
rows.notes: parent: conservation_of_momentum_atomic; **space-strand anchor: ISRO multi-stage separation**

rows.concept: 1D collision conserving BOTH momentum AND kinetic energy. Closed-form solution: v₁' = ((m₁−m₂)v₁ + 2m₂v₂)/(m₁+m₂); v₂' = ((m₂−m₁)v₂ + 2m₁v₁)/(m₁+m₂). Equal-mass case: simple velocity exchange.
rows.notes: MC-G5; **Diamond candidate** — equal-mass-velocity-exchange visualisation

rows.concept: m₁ = m₂: v₁' = v₂; v₂' = v₁. Classic Newton's cradle / billiards visualization.
rows.notes: parent: elastic_collision_1d_atomic

rows.concept: 2D oblique elastic collision: conservation of momentum applied PER COMPONENT (x and y), plus conservation of KE; results in coupled equations. Geometric significance: post-collision velocity directions form right angle for equal masses
rows.notes: MC-G5; JEE-Advanced staple

rows.concept: When equal masses collide elastically with one at rest: post-collision velocity vectors are perpendicular. Classic billiards-shot geometry.
rows.notes: parent: elastic_collision_2d_atomic

rows.concept: Momentum conserved; KE NOT conserved (some converted to heat, sound, deformation). Coefficient of restitution e ∈ [0,1] characterises energy retention
rows.notes: MC-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "real collisions are elastic" → most real collisions are partly inelastic. **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed `coefficient_of_restitution_atomic` from Requires — teach the phenomenon (KE lost) first; e quantifies it (now in Required-by).

rows.concept: Bodies stick together after collision; common post-collision velocity v_f = (m₁v₁ + m₂v₂)/(m₁+m₂); maximum KE-loss case; e = 0
rows.notes: MC-G6; canonical-formula atomic

rows.concept: Two Indian Railways wagons couple on track: bodies stick (Janney coupler engagement) → perfectly inelastic; coupling-and-uncoupling at Mughalsarai/Bandra/Itarsi yards.
rows.notes: parent: perfectly_inelastic_collision_atomic; **transport-strand anchor: Indian Railways**

rows.concept: e = |v_separation|/|v_approach|; e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic, 0 < e < 1 general inelastic. Property of the COLLISION PAIR (not single object)
rows.notes: MC-G7; **cognitive_error_target:** "e is property of one ball" → e is property of collision pair

rows.concept: Ball dropped from height h₀ bounces to h₁ = e²h₀; nth bounce → h_n = e^(2n)h₀; series of decreasing heights
rows.notes: parent: coefficient_of_restitution_atomic; everyday Indian observation

rows.concept: Cricket ball-bat collision: e ≈ 0.45 for hardened-leather + willow; affects six-vs-four trajectory; depends on bat-thickness, ball-condition, swing-speed. Indian cricket-equipment-industry context.
rows.notes: parent: coefficient_of_restitution_atomic; **sports-strand anchor: Indian cricket**

rows.concept: Tsiolkovsky: v − v₀ = u·ln(M₀/M); rocket gains speed by ejecting mass at exhaust-speed u; specific impulse I_sp = u/g₀ measures fuel efficiency
rows.notes: MC-G8; **Diamond candidate** — Tsiolkovsky derivation sim with ISRO mission examples

rows.concept: PSLV: 4 stages (PS1 solid + PS2 liquid + PS3 solid + PS4 liquid); each stage burns + separates (explosion → reverse collision). Total Δv achievable via Tsiolkovsky summed across stages.
rows.notes: parent: rocket_equation_atomic; **space-strand anchor: ISRO PSLV**

rows.concept: I_sp = u/g₀ in seconds; chemical rocket: 250-450 s; ion thruster: 1000-5000 s; ISRO LAM (liquid apogee motor) ~300 s
rows.notes: parent: rocket_equation_atomic

rows.concept: μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂); converts 2-body problem to equivalent 1-body problem of mass μ moving in central potential. Foundation of 2-body collision + Rutherford scattering + Bohr H-atom correction
rows.notes: MC-G9; **cross-cluster bridge atomic** to T47

rows.concept: Alpha + gold-nucleus: μ ≈ m_α since m_Au >> m_α; cross-link to T47 atomic-models
rows.notes: parent: reduced_mass_atomic; cross-link to T47

rows.concept: Rigid body = all internal distances constant; rotation about fixed axis = every point traces a circle of fixed radius around axis
rows.notes: Foundational; defines the rotational system

rows.concept: Pure rotation = points trace circles around fixed axis (axis is stationary). Pure translation = all points have same velocity. General motion = translation + rotation about CoM
rows.notes: parent: rigid_body_rotation_atomic

rows.concept: ω = dθ/dt; α = dω/dt; ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ. **Rotational analog of T6 linear kinematics.**
rows.notes: RM-G3; **cognitive_error_target:** "angular and linear kinematics are different physics" → same math, different variables (mass↔I; F↔τ; v↔ω; a↔α)

rows.concept: Linear velocity of point at radius r: v = ωr. Linear acceleration tangential: a_t = αr. Linear acceleration centripetal: a_c = ω²r. **Three relations bridging linear ↔ angular.**
rows.notes: parent: rotational_kinematics_atomic

rows.concept: I = Σmᵢrᵢ² (discrete); I = ∫r² dm (continuous). I is "rotational mass" — resists change in rotational motion. **DEPENDS ON CHOSEN AXIS.**
rows.notes: RM-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "I is a property of body alone" → I depends on the AXIS

rows.concept: Solid sphere: I = (2/5)MR². Hollow sphere: (2/3)MR². Disc/cylinder about axis: ½MR². Ring: MR². Rod about end: ML²/3. Rod about centre: ML²/12. **Table for V1 reference.**
rows.notes: parent: moment_of_inertia_atomic; **canonical-formulas table**

rows.concept: I_parallel = I_CoM + Md²; moment of inertia about any axis parallel to CoM-axis equals I_CoM + (mass × distance²). Allows computing I about arbitrary axis.
rows.notes: parent: moment_of_inertia_atomic

rows.concept: For PLANAR body in xy-plane: I_z = I_x + I_y. Only valid for thin laminar (planar) bodies; not for 3D solids. **cognitive_error_target:** "perpendicular-axis applies to all bodies" → planar only.
rows.notes: parent: moment_of_inertia_atomic

rows.concept: τ = r × F (vector form); τ = rF sin θ (magnitude); τ is to angular motion what F is to linear motion. SI unit: N·m
rows.notes: RM-G4; **cross-link to T36 magnetic dipole torque**

rows.concept: τ = F × d_perpendicular = F × (r sin θ). Moment arm = perpendicular distance from axis to line-of-action of force. **cognitive_error_target:** "torque = r·F always" → r sin θ matters; force ALONG r contributes ZERO torque
rows.notes: parent: torque_atomic; RM-G12 cognitive_error_target

rows.concept: Pair of equal-opposite forces with separation d: net force = 0; net torque = F·d (independent of axis choice). Door-handle, wrench-on-bolt examples
rows.notes: parent: torque_atomic

rows.concept: τ_net = Iα; central quantitative law of rotational dynamics. Rotational analog of F_net = ma
rows.notes: RM-G5; **Diamond candidate**

rows.concept: Side-by-side table: m↔I, F↔τ, v↔ω, a↔α, F=ma↔τ=Iα, p=mv↔L=Iω, KE=½mv²↔KE=½Iω². **Cognitive scaffold nano.**
rows.notes: parent: tau_eq_i_alpha_atomic; conceptual scaffold

rows.concept: W_torque = ∫τ dθ; rotational KE = ½Iω²; total KE for rolling = ½mv² + ½Iω². Bridges T13 Work-Energy linear treatment
rows.notes: RM-G8

rows.concept: L = Iω (rotational) = r × p (general); SI unit: kg·m²/s; vector quantity; direction by right-hand rule along ω
rows.notes: RM-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "L is a scalar" → L is a vector along the rotation axis

rows.concept: For a single particle: L = r × p; for system: L_total = Σ(rᵢ × pᵢ); axis-independent definition
rows.notes: parent: angular_momentum_atomic

rows.concept: When no external torque acts (τ_ext = 0): L_total = constant. Derivation: τ_ext = dL/dt = 0 → L = const. **Pirouette, planetary Kepler-2, gyroscope-stability all apply**
rows.notes: RM-G6

rows.concept: Ice-skater / Bharatnatyam dancer pirouette: arms pulled in → I decreases → ω increases (L = Iω constant). Indian classical-dance everyday observation
rows.notes: parent: conservation_of_angular_momentum_atomic; **dance/sports-strand anchor: Bharatnatyam + ice-skating + gymnastics**

rows.concept: Geostationary satellites use reaction-wheel-based attitude control: spinning wheel inside satellite changes satellite's angular momentum; ISRO INSAT/GSAT/Cartosat all use this
rows.notes: parent: conservation_of_angular_momentum_atomic; **space-strand anchor: ISRO**

rows.concept: Law of equal areas: planets sweep equal areas in equal times → dA/dt = L/2m = constant (when L conserved under central force). Cross-link to T16 Gravitation
rows.notes: parent: conservation_of_angular_momentum_atomic; cross-link to T16

rows.concept: Rolling without slipping: v_CoM = Rω (constraint); contact-point velocity = 0 instantaneously; STATIC friction (not kinetic) provides the rotational coupling
rows.notes: RM-G7; **cognitive_error_target:** "rolling friction is kinetic" → static (contact-point momentarily at rest)

rows.concept: Rolling-without-slipping condition: v_CoM − Rω = 0 → contact point has zero velocity at any instant. **Diamond visualisation candidate**: trace path of contact point = cycloid
rows.notes: parent: pure_rolling_atomic

rows.concept: KE_total = KE_translation + KE_rotation = ½mv_CoM² + ½Iω² = ½mv² + ½I(v/R)² = ½v²(m + I/R²). Larger I/R² → more KE in rotation
rows.notes: parent: pure_rolling_atomic

rows.concept: Body rolling without slipping down incline angle θ: a_CoM = g sin θ / (1 + I/(mR²)); time-to-bottom depends on I/(mR²) ratio. **Solid sphere < disc < hollow sphere < ring**
rows.notes: RM-G9; **Diamond candidate** — sphere-vs-disc-vs-ring race-down-incline sim

rows.concept: Race down same incline: solid sphere wins (smallest I/mR² = 2/5); disc 2nd (1/2); hollow sphere 3rd (2/3); ring last (1.0). Iconic Class 11 demo
rows.notes: parent: rolling_on_incline_atomic

rows.concept: Disc suspended by wire with torsional rigidity κ: τ = −κθ → I·d²θ/dt² = −κθ → angular SHM with ω² = κ/I; T = 2π√(I/κ)
rows.notes: RM-G10; **bridges to T17 SHM**; cross-link to T37 dipole-in-field angular SHM; NCERT-not-covered (HCV+DCM only)

rows.concept: Wheel with large I stores rotational KE = ½Iω² and smooths angular-velocity fluctuations. Automotive engine flywheel, power-plant turbine inertia, KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) all use this
rows.notes: **Diamond candidate**; industry anchor; cross-link to T26 Thermodynamics IC engines

rows.concept: `F = Gm₁m₂/r²`, central + conservative + medium-independent. The diamond seed.
rows.notes: `F = Gm₁m₂/r²`, central + conservative + medium-independent. The diamond seed.

rows.notes: DCM2 Fig 13.1

rows.notes: DCM2 Fig 13.4 + Example 13.5; ∫dF over rod length

rows.notes: G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²; measured by Cavendish torsion balance

rows.notes: HCV1 §11.2 derivation: `G = kθr²/(Mml)`; "Cavendish weighed the Earth"

rows.notes: "External point sees shell as point-mass at centre" — Newton's Principia result

rows.notes: Equator R 21 km larger than poles → g larger at poles. Geometric, not dynamical.

rows.notes: DCM2 Example 13.8: "rotate Earth fast enough for weight at equator = 0 → T = 1.4 hr"

rows.notes: `v_e = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s` from Earth's surface; 2.3 km/s from Moon

rows.notes: Moon's low v_e (2.3 km/s) < typical gas-molecule thermal speed → atmosphere escapes

rows.notes: `E = -GMm/(2(R+h))`; KE = -E (positive), PE = 2E (negative); total negative → bound orbit

rows.notes: E<0 elliptical/circular bound; E=0 parabolic escape; E>0 hyperbolic escape

rows.notes: T = 24 hr → h ≈ 35,800 km; equatorial orbit only; INSAT series.

rows.notes: Force along line of centres → angular momentum conserved → Kepler law of areas

rows.notes: DCM2 problem class; NCERT Example 8.4 (two spheres M and 4M at 6R separation → neutral point at 2R from M)

rows.concept: Net force = 0 by symmetry when all vertex masses equal; ≠ 0 if symmetry broken (DCM2 Examples 13.3/13.4)
rows.notes: Net force = 0 by symmetry when all vertex masses equal; ≠ 0 if symmetry broken (DCM2 Examples 13.3/13.4)

rows.notes: `F = GMm/(a(l+a))`; cannot assume rod as point at centre

rows.notes: DCM2 Example 13.6: `F = √3·GMm/(8a²)` for ring at √3·a from sphere centre

rows.concept: The defining condition F = −kx, with examples (spring-block, projection of UCM). Core of the topic.
rows.notes: The defining condition F = −kx, with examples (spring-block, projection of UCM). Core of the topic.

rows.concept: Definition: motion repeating at fixed T. Non-SHM examples (rolling ball, parabolic bounce). Per S-G1 — not its own atomic.
rows.notes: Definition: motion repeating at fixed T. Non-SHM examples (rolling ball, parabolic bounce). Per S-G1 — not its own atomic.

rows.concept: The minus sign means F is always toward mean position. Visual: arrow flips when block crosses x=0.
rows.notes: The minus sign means F is always toward mean position. Visual: arrow flips when block crosses x=0.

rows.concept: k = F/x = stiffness. Units N/m. k=mω².
rows.notes: k = F/x = stiffness. Units N/m. k=mω².

rows.concept: a = −ω²x. From a = F/m + F = −kx, ω² = k/m. Maximum at extremes, zero at mean.
rows.notes: a = −ω²x. From a = F/m + F = −kx, ω² = k/m. Maximum at extremes, zero at mean.

rows.concept: ω = √(k/m), units rad/s, ω = 2π/T = 2πf. Distinct from linear ω of UCM but same units (per HCV1 12.4 (c)).
rows.notes: ω = √(k/m), units rad/s, ω = 2π/T = 2πf. Distinct from linear ω of UCM but same units (per HCV1 12.4 (c)).

rows.concept: a−x is a line through origin, slope −ω². DCM2 14.2 (Fig 14.5) canonical figure.
rows.notes: a−x is a line through origin, slope −ω². DCM2 14.2 (Fig 14.5) canonical figure.

rows.concept: v(x) = ±ω√(A²−x²). At extremes v=0, at mean v=±ωA. The v−x ellipse.
rows.notes: v(x) = ±ω√(A²−x²). At extremes v=0, at mean v=±ωA. The v−x ellipse.

rows.concept: v_max = ωA, occurs at x=0. Critical for energy bookkeeping.
rows.notes: v_max = ωA, occurs at x=0. Critical for energy bookkeeping.

rows.concept: (v/ωA)² + (x/A)² = 1, an ellipse in v−x plane. DCM2 Fig 14.7.
rows.notes: (v/ωA)² + (x/A)² = 1, an ellipse in v−x plane. DCM2 Fig 14.7.

rows.concept: x(t) = A sin(ωt + φ) or A cos(ωt + φ). Solution of d²x/dt² = −ω²x. T = 2π/ω.
rows.notes: x(t) = A sin(ωt + φ) or A cos(ωt + φ). Solution of d²x/dt² = −ω²x. T = 2π/ω.

rows.concept: We don't solve d²x/dt² = −ω²x explicitly; we recognize sin/cos as general solution. NCERT 14.3 eq 14.4.
rows.notes: We don't solve d²x/dt² = −ω²x explicitly; we recognize sin/cos as general solution. NCERT 14.3 eq 14.4.

rows.concept: Argument (ωt+φ) is phase; φ is phase constant set by initial conditions. HCV1 §12.4(d).
rows.notes: Argument (ωt+φ) is phase; φ is phase constant set by initial conditions. HCV1 §12.4(d).

rows.concept: A sin(ωt + π/2) = A cos(ωt). Choice depends on convenience. HCV1 §12.4(e).
rows.notes: A sin(ωt + π/2) = A cos(ωt). Choice depends on convenience. HCV1 §12.4(e).

rows.concept: Foot of perpendicular from P on x-axis executes SHM. x = A cos ωt, y = A sin ωt. NCERT §14.4, HCV1 §12.5. Cross-topic bridge T10→T17.
rows.notes: Foot of perpendicular from P on x-axis executes SHM. x = A cos ωt, y = A sin ωt. NCERT §14.4, HCV1 §12.5. Cross-topic bridge T10→T17.

rows.concept: The auxiliary circle (radius A) and uniformly rotating particle P′. Visualization-only construct.
rows.notes: The auxiliary circle (radius A) and uniformly rotating particle P′. Visualization-only construct.

rows.concept: x-projection = A cos ωt, y-projection = A sin ωt. Same A and ω, phase differ by π/2. Setup for Lissajous (A29).
rows.notes: x-projection = A cos ωt, y-projection = A sin ωt. Same A and ω, phase differ by π/2. Setup for Lissajous (A29).

rows.concept: E = ½kA² = ½mω²A². Independent of t. Cross-topic bridge T13→T17.
rows.notes: E = ½kA² = ½mω²A². Independent of t. Cross-topic bridge T13→T17.

rows.concept: KE = ½m·ω²(A²−x²) = ½k(A²−x²). Max at mean, zero at extremes.
rows.notes: KE = ½m·ω²(A²−x²) = ½k(A²−x²). Max at mean, zero at extremes.

rows.concept: U = ½kx². Parabola in U−x plot. Period of U is T/2 (per NCERT 14.7).
rows.notes: U = ½kx². Parabola in U−x plot. Period of U is T/2 (per NCERT 14.7).

rows.concept: KE oscillates 2× per cycle of x — common JEE trick. NCERT §14.7.
rows.notes: KE oscillates 2× per cycle of x — common JEE trick. NCERT §14.7.

rows.concept: E flat line, U parabola opening up, KE parabola opening down. They sum to E. Canonical figure NCERT Fig 14.16(b), DCM2 Fig 14.9.
rows.notes: E flat line, U parabola opening up, KE parabola opening down. They sum to E. Canonical figure NCERT Fig 14.16(b), DCM2 Fig 14.9.

rows.concept: At x = A/2: KE = 3E/4, PE = E/4 (or = 3:1 ratio). Frequently typed JEE pattern. DCM2 Ex 14.5.
rows.notes: At x = A/2: KE = 3E/4, PE = E/4 (or = 3:1 ratio). Frequently typed JEE pattern. DCM2 Ex 14.5.

rows.concept: Per S-G2. Four canonical (x₀, v₀) combinations → four canonical x(t) forms. DCM2 Table 14.1.
rows.notes: Per S-G2. Four canonical (x₀, v₀) combinations → four canonical x(t) forms. DCM2 Table 14.1.

rows.concept: t=0: x=0, v=+ωA → x = A sin ωt.
rows.notes: t=0: x=0, v=+ωA → x = A sin ωt.

rows.concept: t=0: x=0, v=−ωA → x = −A sin ωt.
rows.notes: t=0: x=0, v=−ωA → x = −A sin ωt.

rows.concept: t=0: x=+A, v=0 → x = A cos ωt.
rows.notes: t=0: x=+A, v=0 → x = A cos ωt.

rows.concept: t=0: x=−A, v=0 → x = −A cos ωt.
rows.notes: t=0: x=−A, v=0 → x = −A cos ωt.

rows.concept: Time from mean → A/2 is T/12; mean → A/√2 is T/8; mean → A√3/2 is T/6. Canonical timing-trick pattern. HCV1 W.Ex 4, DCM2 Ex 14.9.
rows.notes: Time from mean → A/2 is T/12; mean → A/√2 is T/8; mean → A√3/2 is T/6. Canonical timing-trick pattern. HCV1 W.Ex 4, DCM2 Ex 14.9.

rows.concept: sin ωt = 1/2 ⇒ ωt = π/6 ⇒ t = T/12.
rows.notes: sin ωt = 1/2 ⇒ ωt = π/6 ⇒ t = T/12.

rows.concept: Given (x₁,v₁) and (x₂,v₂): use v² = ω²(A²−x²) to solve A and ω. Common JEE typed problem.
rows.notes: Given (x₁,v₁) and (x₂,v₂): use v² = ω²(A²−x²) to solve A and ω. Common JEE typed problem.

rows.concept: x leads v by π/2, leads a by π (out of phase). NCERT Fig 14.13. Important for graph-matching MCQ.
rows.notes: x leads v by π/2, leads a by π (out of phase). NCERT Fig 14.13. Important for graph-matching MCQ.

rows.concept: T = 2π√(m/k). The canonical entry pattern. NCERT §14.8.1, HCV1 §12.2.
rows.notes: T = 2π√(m/k). The canonical entry pattern. NCERT §14.8.1, HCV1 §12.2.

rows.concept: Per S-G3. The umbrella for parallel + series combined-spring systems.
rows.notes: Per S-G3. The umbrella for parallel + series combined-spring systems.

rows.concept: Two springs both attached to block side-by-side: k_eff = k₁ + k₂. NCERT Ex 14.6 (two springs k each on either side, T = 2π√(m/2k)).
rows.notes: Two springs both attached to block side-by-side: k_eff = k₁ + k₂. NCERT Ex 14.6 (two springs k each on either side, T = 2π√(m/2k)).

rows.concept: End-to-end springs: 1/k_eff = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂.
rows.notes: End-to-end springs: 1/k_eff = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂.

rows.concept: Hanging spring: mean position shifts by mg/k but T is unchanged. HCV1 W.Ex 5. Common misconception trap.
rows.notes: Hanging spring: mean position shifts by mg/k but T is unchanged. HCV1 W.Ex 5. Common misconception trap.

rows.concept: Block compressed by spring on one side and stretched on other → both forces toward mean → F = −2kx → T = 2π√(m/2k). NCERT Ex 14.6.
rows.notes: Block compressed by spring on one side and stretched on other → both forces toward mean → F = −2kx → T = 2π√(m/2k). NCERT Ex 14.6.

rows.concept: Block of mass m with speed v hits spring → ½mv² = ½kA² → A = v√(m/k). HCV1 W.Ex 9. Connects T13 (KE) → T17 (amplitude).
rows.notes: Block of mass m with speed v hits spring → ½mv² = ½kA² → A = v√(m/k). HCV1 W.Ex 9. Connects T13 (KE) → T17 (amplitude).

rows.concept: T = 2π√(L/g). Per S-G4 — distinct atomic from physical pendulum. NCERT §14.8.2, HCV1 §12.8. **Indian-context anchor:** "What length of pendulum ticks seconds?" — NCERT Ex 14.9, answer L = 1 m. **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed A21 (angular-SHM eqn) from Requires — the simple pendulum is derived directly from linear F=−kx + small-angle approx, not from the general angular-SHM equation. Leaves clean A18→A20→A21.
rows.notes: T = 2π√(L/g). Per S-G4 — distinct atomic from physical pendulum. NCERT §14.8.2, HCV1 §12.8. **Indian-context anchor:** "What length of pendulum ticks seconds?" — NCERT Ex 14.9, answer L = 1 m. **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed A21 (angular-SHM eqn) from Requires — the simple pendulum is derived directly from linear F=−kx + small-angle approx, not from the general angular-SHM equation. Leaves clean A18→A20→A21.

rows.concept: sin θ ≈ θ for θ < 0.349 rad (20°). Justifies linearization. NCERT Table 14.1 + safety bound at 50° for ≤5% error.
rows.notes: sin θ ≈ θ for θ < 0.349 rad (20°). Justifies linearization. NCERT Table 14.1 + safety bound at 50° for ≤5% error.

rows.concept: T does not depend on bob mass or (small) amplitude — the Galileo observation. Per NCERT 14.8.2 introductory paragraph.
rows.notes: T does not depend on bob mass or (small) amplitude — the Galileo observation. Per NCERT 14.8.2 introductory paragraph.

rows.concept: g = 4π²L/T². Lab procedure: measure 20 oscillations to reduce stopwatch error. HCV1 §12.8 (Determination of g).
rows.notes: g = 4π²L/T². Lab procedure: measure 20 oscillations to reduce stopwatch error. HCV1 §12.8 (Determination of g).

rows.concept: Per S-G4. Rigid body suspended through point O: T = 2π√(I/mgL). HCV1 §12.9. Rod-as-pendulum, meter-stick-pendulum patterns.
rows.notes: Per S-G4. Rigid body suspended through point O: T = 2π√(I/mgL). HCV1 §12.9. Rod-as-pendulum, meter-stick-pendulum patterns.

rows.concept: Per S-G5. Angular equivalent of F=−kx: Γ = −kθ. T = 2π√(I/k). HCV1 §12.7. **Indian-context anchor:** hanging umbrella oscillations.
rows.notes: Per S-G5. Angular equivalent of F=−kx: Γ = −kθ. T = 2π√(I/k). HCV1 §12.7. **Indian-context anchor:** hanging umbrella oscillations.

rows.concept: ω² = k/I. Analogous to k/m in linear SHM.
rows.notes: ω² = k/I. Analogous to k/m in linear SHM.

rows.concept: Disc twisted on wire: torsional constant k, T = 2π√(I/k). HCV1 §12.10 (Fig 12.14). Used for measuring torsional rigidity.
rows.notes: Disc twisted on wire: torsional constant k, T = 2π√(I/k). HCV1 §12.10 (Fig 12.14). Used for measuring torsional rigidity.

rows.concept: F_d = −bv. Total force on block: m d²x/dt² + b dx/dt + kx = 0. NCERT §14.9.
rows.notes: F_d = −bv. Total force on block: m d²x/dt² + b dx/dt + kx = 0. NCERT §14.9.

rows.concept: b/√(km) determines damping regime. NCERT Eq 14.34 onward.
rows.notes: b/√(km) determines damping regime. NCERT Eq 14.34 onward.

rows.concept: x(t) = A e^(−bt/2m) cos(ω′t + φ), ω′ = √(k/m − b²/4m²). Amplitude decays exponentially. NCERT Fig 14.20.
rows.notes: x(t) = A e^(−bt/2m) cos(ω′t + φ), ω′ = √(k/m − b²/4m²). Amplitude decays exponentially. NCERT Fig 14.20.

rows.concept: T_{1/2,amp} = (ln 2)·2m/b. From e^(−bt/2m) = 1/2. NCERT Ex 14.10.
rows.notes: T_{1/2,amp} = (ln 2)·2m/b. From e^(−bt/2m) = 1/2. NCERT Ex 14.10.

rows.concept: E ∝ A² → T_{1/2,energy} = T_{1/2,amp}/2. Common JEE distinction. NCERT Ex 14.10(c).
rows.notes: E ∝ A² → T_{1/2,energy} = T_{1/2,amp}/2. Common JEE distinction. NCERT Ex 14.10(c).

rows.concept: When b²/4m² = k/m, motion just barely returns without oscillating. HCV1 §12.12.
rows.notes: When b²/4m² = k/m, motion just barely returns without oscillating. HCV1 §12.12.

rows.concept: External F₀ cos(ω_d t) drives system. Eventually x(t) = A_d cos(ω_d t + φ), at driver frequency ω_d, not natural ω. NCERT §14.10.
rows.notes: External F₀ cos(ω_d t) drives system. Eventually x(t) = A_d cos(ω_d t + φ), at driver frequency ω_d, not natural ω. NCERT §14.10.

rows.concept: Amplitude A_d maximum when ω_d ≈ ω. Peak sharpness ↑ when damping ↓. NCERT Fig 14.21. **Indian-context anchor:** "Soldiers go out of step while crossing a bridge — same reason an earthquake will not cause uniform damage to all buildings, even of same strength" (direct NCERT 14.10 closing paragraph).
rows.notes: Amplitude A_d maximum when ω_d ≈ ω. Peak sharpness ↑ when damping ↓. NCERT Fig 14.21. **Indian-context anchor:** "Soldiers go out of step while crossing a bridge — same reason an earthquake will not cause uniform damage to all buildings, even of same strength" (direct NCERT 14.10 closing paragraph).

rows.concept: 5 pendulums on common rope: only the one with matching length picks up large amplitude. NCERT Fig 14.22.
rows.notes: 5 pendulums on common rope: only the one with matching length picks up large amplitude. NCERT Fig 14.22.

rows.concept: A sin ωt + B cos ωt = D sin(ωt + φ) where D = √(A²+B²), tan φ = B/A. The trig-identity bedrock. NCERT Eq 14.3c, HCV1 §12.11.
rows.notes: A sin ωt + B cos ωt = D sin(ωt + φ) where D = √(A²+B²), tan φ = B/A. The trig-identity bedrock. NCERT Eq 14.3c, HCV1 §12.11.

rows.concept: Per S-G6 split (A). Two SHMs along same axis with phase difference δ: x = A sin(ωt + ε) where A = √(A₁²+A₂²+2A₁A₂ cos δ). HCV1 §12.11(A).
rows.notes: Per S-G6 split (A). Two SHMs along same axis with phase difference δ: x = A sin(ωt + ε) where A = √(A₁²+A₂²+2A₁A₂ cos δ). HCV1 §12.11(A).

rows.concept: δ=0: A = A₁ + A₂. Constructive.
rows.notes: δ=0: A = A₁ + A₂. Constructive.

rows.concept: δ=π: A = \|A₁ − A₂\|. Destructive. Zero amplitude if A₁=A₂.
rows.notes: δ=π: A = \|A₁ − A₂\|. Destructive. Zero amplitude if A₁=A₂.

rows.concept: Represent each SHM as a vector of magnitude A_i at angle δ_i. Resultant by parallelogram. HCV1 §12.11 Vector Method, Fig 12.16.
rows.notes: Represent each SHM as a vector of magnitude A_i at angle δ_i. Resultant by parallelogram. HCV1 §12.11 Vector Method, Fig 12.16.

rows.concept: Per S-G6 split (B). Two SHMs along perpendicular axes (x and y). General path is ellipse: x²/A₁² + y²/A₂² − 2xy cos δ/(A₁A₂) = sin²δ. HCV1 §12.11(B).
rows.notes: Per S-G6 split (B). Two SHMs along perpendicular axes (x and y). General path is ellipse: x²/A₁² + y²/A₂² − 2xy cos δ/(A₁A₂) = sin²δ. HCV1 §12.11(B).

rows.concept: δ=0: y = (A₂/A₁) x. Line through origin, slope +A₂/A₁. Fig 12.19.
rows.notes: δ=0: y = (A₂/A₁) x. Line through origin, slope +A₂/A₁. Fig 12.19.

rows.concept: δ=π: y = −(A₂/A₁) x. Line, negative slope. Fig 12.20.
rows.notes: δ=π: y = −(A₂/A₁) x. Line, negative slope. Fig 12.20.

rows.concept: δ=π/2: x²/A₁² + y²/A₂² = 1. Standard ellipse along the coordinate axes. Fig 12.21.
rows.notes: δ=π/2: x²/A₁² + y²/A₂² = 1. Standard ellipse along the coordinate axes. Fig 12.21.

rows.concept: A₁=A₂ AND δ=π/2: x²+y² = A². Pure circle. (Foreshadow of UCM→SHM bridge.)
rows.notes: A₁=A₂ AND δ=π/2: x²+y² = A². Pure circle. (Foreshadow of UCM→SHM bridge.)

rows.concept: Rigid-body assumption (T11-T17) is an idealisation; real bodies deform under applied forces; elasticity is the theory of small-deformation regime
rows.notes: Conceptual atomic; bridge from Mechanics

rows.concept: Stress = F/A (force per unit area; SI: Pa = N/m²). Strain = ΔL/L (dimensionless ratio of deformation to original dimension). **Three stress types: tensile (axial), shear (tangential), hydraulic (uniform pressure)**.
rows.notes: EL-G1; foundational; **Diamond candidate**

rows.concept: Force F along axis: F/A acts along normal. Tensile = pulling apart; compressive = pushing together. Same magnitude/formula, opposite directions.
rows.notes: parent: stress_strain_atomic

rows.concept: Force F tangential to surface area A: F/A acts in plane. Pure shear deforms a rectangle into a parallelogram. Cross-link to T15 torque (rotational analog).
rows.notes: parent: stress_strain_atomic; **cross-cluster link to T15**

rows.concept: Uniform pressure P acts on all surfaces of body: volumetric stress = P (SI: Pa). Strain = ΔV/V. Applies in deep oceans, hydraulic presses, atmospheric compression.
rows.notes: parent: stress_strain_atomic

rows.concept: Within elastic limit: stress ∝ strain → stress = E × strain (where E is the relevant modulus). Linear regime only.
rows.notes: EL-G2; **cognitive_error_target:** "Hooke's law applies always" → only within elastic limit

rows.concept: Graphical representation showing: proportional limit (Hooke's law) → elastic limit → yield point → plastic regime → ultimate stress → fracture. Different materials show distinct curves (steel: brittle; rubber: ultra-elastic; glass: brittle no-yield)
rows.notes: EL-G3; **Diamond candidate** — material-comparison sim

rows.concept: (1) Proportional limit: Hooke holds. (2) Elastic limit: deformation reverses on unloading; non-linear in this band. (3) Yield point: plastic deformation begins. (4) Plastic regime: permanent deformation. (5) Ultimate stress: peak before fracture. **Cognitive scaffold nano.**
rows.notes: parent: stress_strain_curve_atomic

rows.concept: Ductile (large plastic regime): copper, aluminum, gold — drawn into wire. Brittle (negligible plastic regime): glass, cast iron — shatter without warning. Indian materials industry context.
rows.notes: parent: stress_strain_curve_atomic

rows.concept: Y = (F/A) / (ΔL/L) = stress / longitudinal strain; characterises tensile/compressive deformation. **Industrial values:** steel ~200 GPa, aluminum ~70 GPa, brass ~91 GPa, rubber ~0.01-0.1 GPa
rows.notes: EL-G4; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "Y depends on size of body" → Y is material property only; geometry cancels

rows.concept: Steel Y ≈ 200 GPa (high stiffness; structural). Aluminum Y ≈ 70 GPa (lighter; aerospace). Rubber Y ≈ 0.01-0.1 GPa (extremely flexible; tires + seals). **Materials-selection table.**
rows.notes: parent: youngs_modulus_atomic; **industry anchor: Indian construction + automotive**

rows.concept: Indian Railways rail tracks: high-carbon-steel Y ≈ 210 GPa; spec'd to specific elongation under thermal expansion + dynamic load. Tata Steel + SAIL manufacture per IR specs.
rows.notes: parent: youngs_modulus_atomic; **industry-strand anchor: Tata Steel + SAIL + Indian Railways**

rows.concept: η = (F/A) / (Δx/L) = shear stress / shear strain; characterises shear deformation. Typically 0.3-0.5 × Y for metals
rows.notes: EL-G5; **bridges to T15 torsional pendulum**

rows.concept: Torsion of cylindrical wire by torque τ: angle of twist φ = τL / (η · I_polar); used in torsional pendulum + torsion balance (Cavendish experiment for G measurement)
rows.notes: parent: modulus_of_rigidity_atomic; cross-link to T15 + T16 (Cavendish G)

rows.concept: K = −V(dP/dV) = −(volumetric stress) / (volumetric strain). Negative sign because volume DECREASES as pressure INCREASES. Compressibility = 1/K
rows.notes: EL-G6; **closes T27 forward-edge** (mean_free_path → bulk modulus microscopic)

rows.concept: Solids: K ~10^11 Pa (steel ~160 GPa). Liquids: K ~10^9 Pa (water ~2.2 GPa). Gases: K varies with process; ideal gas isothermal K = P; adiabatic K = γP. **Cross-cluster bridge to T27 (ideal gas K = P)**
rows.notes: parent: bulk_modulus_atomic; **closes T27 mean-free-path → bulk-modulus forward**

rows.concept: Indian Navy submarines (INS Arihant + INS Kalvari) operate at depths ~200-400 m with hydrostatic pressure ~20-40 bar; hull design uses bulk modulus to predict deformation
rows.notes: parent: bulk_modulus_atomic; **defence/navy-strand anchor: Indian Navy**

rows.concept: σ = −(transverse strain) / (longitudinal strain). When body stretched longitudinally, transverse dimensions shrink slightly. Dimensionless; typical values 0.2-0.5
rows.notes: EL-G7; NCERT-light; **cognitive_error_target:** "Poisson's ratio = 0.5 always" → varies 0.2-0.5 by material (cork ~0; steel ~0.3; rubber ~0.5)

rows.concept: Cork ≈ 0 (no transverse change). Steel ≈ 0.30. Brass ≈ 0.34. Aluminum ≈ 0.33. Rubber ≈ 0.50 (incompressible volume). Glass ≈ 0.21.
rows.notes: parent: poissons_ratio_atomic; cognitive-scaffold nano

rows.concept: Energy stored in deformed body within elastic limit: U = ½ × stress × strain × volume = ½ × (F/A) × (ΔL/L) × A·L = ½·F·ΔL. Bridges to T17 SHM spring elastic-PE (½kx²) and T13 Work-Energy general
rows.notes: EL-G8; **bridges to T17 SHM + T13 Work-Energy**

rows.concept: For spring (Hooke's-law system): U = ½kx². Identical form to ½·stress·strain·V because spring IS a Hooke's-law system with effective E·A/L = k
rows.notes: parent: elastic_pe_atomic; cross-link to T11+T17

rows.concept: Beam loading, cantilever bending, bridge engineering all use elasticity: deflection ∝ load/Y; safety factor = ultimate stress / working stress. **Indian civil engineering context**
rows.notes: EL-G10; V1.2-deferred (NCERT-light + JEE-Advanced); **bridge to engineering**

rows.concept: L&T Construction (Mumbai Sea Link, Bandra-Worli, Bogibeel Bridge): structural-steel Y critical for deflection prediction; safety factor 2-3× ultimate stress
rows.notes: parent: construction_engineering_applications_atomic; **industry-strand anchor: L&T + Indian bridge engineering**

rows.concept: DRDO + Indian armed forces armor: composites with high Y per unit weight; ceramic-metal-polymer combinations; Arjun tank + Tejas LCA airframe applications
rows.notes: parent: construction_engineering_applications_atomic; **defence/aerospace-strand anchor: DRDO**

rows.concept: Wave = propagating disturbance. **Transverse:** particle displacement ⊥ to propagation (string, light, water surface). **Longitudinal:** particle displacement ∥ to propagation (sound, pressure). All waves transport energy + momentum WITHOUT net mass transfer.
rows.notes: WE-G6; **Diamond candidate**

rows.concept: Transverse: shake one end of long string → pulse travels along string while each particle moves up-down only. Classroom demo + lab.
rows.notes: parent: transverse_vs_longitudinal_atomic

rows.concept: Slinky compression-rarefaction = longitudinal wave demo; Indian classroom physics-lab standard.
rows.notes: parent: transverse_vs_longitudinal_atomic

rows.concept: Surface water waves: particles trace small circles → mixed transverse + longitudinal. Indian coastal observation (Marina Beach, Goa).
rows.notes: parent: transverse_vs_longitudinal_atomic

rows.concept: Sinusoidal travelling wave: y(x, t) = A sin(kx − ωt + φ). Right-moving: argument (kx − ωt). Left-moving: (kx + ωt). Phase velocity v = ω/k.
rows.notes: WE-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "particles travel with wave" → particles oscillate in place

rows.concept: Pulse = single disturbance (e.g., one shake of string). Continuous wave = repeated sinusoidal source. Both obey wave equation; pulse = sum-of-sinusoids via Fourier (bridge to T22).
rows.notes: parent: travelling_wave_function_atomic

rows.concept: Wave parameters: amplitude A, wavelength λ, period T, frequency f = 1/T, angular frequency ω = 2πf, wave-number k = 2π/λ. Universal relation v = fλ = ω/k.
rows.notes: WE-G4

rows.concept: Maximum displacement from equilibrium. Units: m (transverse) or m (longitudinal displacement) or Pa (sound pressure amplitude).
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Spatial period: distance between consecutive crests (or any 2π phase-matched points). λ = v/f.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Temporal period T = time for one full cycle; f = 1/T = cycles/sec. Hz = s⁻¹.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: k = 2π/λ (rad/m); ω = 2π/T (rad/s). Both phase-rate quantities; central to wave-equation.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Second-order PDE: ∂²y/∂t² = v²·∂²y/∂x². Travelling wave y(x±vt) is general solution (d'Alembert). **Linear PDE → superposition holds (foundation for T22).**
rows.notes: WE-G3; **bridges to T22 + T38**

rows.concept: Substitute y = A sin(kx − ωt) into PDE → ω² = v²k² → v = ω/k. Cognitive scaffold for derivation.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Speed of transverse wave on stretched string: v = √(T/μ); T = tension (N), μ = linear mass density (kg/m). Derived from Newton's 2nd law on small arc. **Industry-anchored: sitar/tabla/guitar string tuning, Indian Railways rail-vibration diagnostics.**
rows.notes: WE-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **closes T11 + T21 forward-edges**

rows.concept: Sitar, tabla, sarod, guitar tuning: increase tension T → higher v → higher f (for fixed L). Indian classical music + Western. **Healthcare bridge:** ultrasound transducers also use string-resonance principles.
rows.notes: parent; **consumer/cultural-strand anchor: Indian musical instruments + ITC Sangeet Research Academy**

rows.concept: Indian Railways uses ultrasonic rail-flaw detection: travelling-wave reflections reveal internal cracks. Tata Steel + IR Research Designs & Standards Organisation (RDSO Lucknow) maintain testing standards.
rows.notes: parent; **transport/industry-strand anchor: Indian Railways RDSO + Tata Steel**

rows.concept: v_particle = ∂y/∂t = −Aω cos(kx − ωt) — oscillates ±Aω. v_wave = ω/k — constant phase-velocity. **Two fundamentally different quantities; common JEE confusion.**
rows.notes: WE-G7; **cognitive_error_target:** "v_particle = v_wave" — both quantities derived separately + visualised

rows.concept: For non-dispersive medium: v_phase = v_group. For dispersive (e.g., deep water, glass-prism light): v_group can differ → wave packet envelope moves at v_group. **V2 extension; flagged.**
rows.notes: parent; V2 deferred

rows.concept: Power transmitted: P = ½μvω²A². Energy density ∝ A²f². Bridges T13 Work-Energy (energy transported through fluid/string) + T17 SHM elastic-PE.
rows.notes: WE-G8; **cross-cluster bridge to T13 + T17**

rows.concept: Wave intensity I = P/A ∝ A²ω² → doubling amplitude quadruples intensity. Critical to T23 sound (dB scale) + T44 wave optics (Malus law variation).
rows.notes: parent; bridges to T23 + T44

rows.concept: Wave at boundary: **fixed end** → π phase change (crest reflects as trough). **Free end** → 0 phase change. Boundary impedance determines partial reflection + transmission. **Sets up T22 standing waves.**
rows.notes: WE-G9; **cognitive_error_target:** "phase change always at reflection" → only at fixed/denser-medium; opens T22

rows.concept: Wave on light string hitting junction with heavy string: partial reflection (phase-inverted) + partial transmission (in-phase). Classroom demo.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: T19 reflection mechanism extends to T42 TIR + T50 optical-fibre communication (BSNL + ISRO satellite-to-ground).
rows.notes: parent; **telecom-strand anchor: BSNL + ISRO**

rows.concept: Pressure in fluid: P = F/A; depth-dependence P = P₀ + ρgh (hydrostatic). Acts equally in all directions at a point. SI: Pa = N/m². Atmospheric pressure ≈ 10⁵ Pa.
rows.notes: FM-G1; foundational

rows.concept: Pressure increases linearly with depth: P(h) = P₀ + ρgh. Indian-Navy submarine context: 10 m water = 1 atm; INS Arihant at 300 m ≈ 31 atm.
rows.notes: parent: fluid_pressure_atomic; cross-link T18 deep_ocean nano

rows.concept: Torricelli mercury barometer: 760 mm Hg = 1 atm = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa. Indian meteorology stations (IMD network) measure surface pressure for weather prediction.
rows.notes: parent: fluid_pressure_atomic

rows.concept: Pressure applied at any point in enclosed incompressible fluid transmits undiminished to every other point. F_out/A_out = F_in/A_in → mechanical advantage = A_out/A_in.
rows.notes: FM-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "pressure increases force proportionally to area" → pressure identical, FORCE scales with area

rows.concept: Tata Motors + Ashok Leyland workshop hydraulic-lift systems use Pascal's law. Maruti/Hyundai service stations India-wide: 100 kg force on 10 cm² piston → 10,000 kg lift on 1 m² piston.
rows.notes: parent: pascals_law_atomic; **industry/consumer-strand anchor: Indian automotive service**

rows.concept: JCB India earthmovers + Tata trucks + Indian Railways braking systems use hydraulic-pressure transmission. JCB Pune manufactures excavators with Pascal-law hydraulics.
rows.notes: parent: pascals_law_atomic; **industry-strand anchor: JCB India + Tata Motors**

rows.concept: Object immersed in fluid experiences upward buoyant force equal to weight of fluid displaced: F_b = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g. Floats if ρ_object < ρ_fluid (effective density via shape).
rows.notes: FM-G3; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "heavier objects sink" → only if DENSER than fluid; iron ship floats via hull-shape lowering effective density

rows.concept: Steel hull of cargo ship: total density (steel + air interior) << water density. INS Vikrant aircraft carrier displacement 45,000 tonnes — floats per Archimedes. **Cognitive scaffold nano.**
rows.notes: parent: archimedes_buoyancy_atomic; **defence/navy-strand anchor: INS Vikrant**

rows.concept: Indian Navy submarines (INS Arihant, Kalvari class) adjust buoyancy by flooding/blowing ballast tanks — controls effective ρ.
rows.notes: parent: archimedes_buoyancy_atomic; **defence/navy-strand anchor: Indian Navy**

rows.concept: Hot air at 100°C has ρ ≈ 0.95 kg/m³ vs cold air at 25°C ρ ≈ 1.18 kg/m³ → buoyancy lift. Indian Hot Air Balloon Festival (Pushkar Rajasthan).
rows.notes: parent: archimedes_buoyancy_atomic; **consumer/tourism-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Mass conservation in incompressible flow: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂. Volume-flow-rate Q = Av is constant along tube.
rows.notes: FM-G4; prerequisite for Bernoulli

rows.concept: Ganges narrows at Rishikesh + Haridwar gorges → flow speeds up; widens at delta → slows. Same flow rate.
rows.notes: parent: continuity_equation_atomic; **geography/agriculture-strand anchor: Indian rivers**

rows.concept: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant along streamline (incompressible + non-viscous + steady flow). Pressure DROPS where velocity rises.
rows.notes: FM-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "Bernoulli applies to any flow" → only incompressible + non-viscous + steady + along-streamline

rows.concept: HAL Tejas + Boeing 787 Air India + IndiGo A320 wing: curved upper surface → faster airflow → lower pressure → net upward lift. **Approximation only** (Bernoulli partially explains; angle-of-attack also critical).
rows.notes: parent: bernoullis_principle_atomic; **aviation-strand anchor: HAL + Air India + IndiGo**

rows.concept: Pipe narrowing → higher v → lower P → manometer reading gives flow rate. Used in Indian petroleum (IOCL refineries) + water-supply (Hindustan Tin Works) + medical IV drips.
rows.notes: parent: bernoullis_principle_atomic; **industry + healthcare-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Asian Paints + Berger Paints spray-gun atomisers; horizontal high-v airflow creates low pressure that sucks paint up nozzle. Also IPL stadium watering sprinklers.
rows.notes: parent: bernoullis_principle_atomic; **consumer/industry-strand anchor**

rows.concept: F = ηA(dv/dy); shear stress = η × velocity-gradient. SI: Pa·s. η_water(20°C) ≈ 10⁻³ Pa·s; η_honey ≈ 10 Pa·s; η_air ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. Temperature-dependent (liquids: η ↓ as T ↑; gases: η ↑ as T ↑).
rows.notes: FM-G6; **cross-cluster bridge to T18 shear stress**; **cognitive_error_target:** "viscosity = thickness" → viscosity is rate-dependent shear resistance; T-dependent

rows.concept: Indian Oil (IOCL) + HP + BP engine-oil SAE grades: 10W-30, 5W-40 — multi-grade oils with η-T behaviour spec'd for Indian climate (40-45°C summer).
rows.notes: parent: viscosity_atomic; **industry/consumer-strand anchor: IOCL**

rows.concept: Drag on small sphere in viscous fluid: F_drag = 6πηrv (low-Re limit). Terminal v: v_t = (2/9)·(ρ_sphere − ρ_fluid)gr²/η when drag = net weight.
rows.notes: FM-G7

rows.concept: Monsoon raindrop (r ≈ 2 mm) reaches v_t ≈ 6-10 m/s in seconds; without air resistance would fall at >100 m/s from 1 km cloud. **Indian monsoon context.**
rows.notes: parent: stokes_terminal_velocity_atomic; **agriculture/meteorology-strand anchor: IMD + monsoon**

rows.concept: DRDO + Indian Army parachute design (ADRDE Agra) uses Stokes/quadratic-drag scaling for terminal v ≈ 5 m/s landing speed.
rows.notes: parent: stokes_terminal_velocity_atomic; **defence-strand anchor: DRDO ADRDE**

rows.concept: Re = ρvD/η; dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Re < 2000: laminar; Re > 4000: turbulent; 2000-4000: transition. Predicts onset of turbulence.
rows.notes: FM-G8; **cognitive_error_target:** "Re predicts flow type uniquely" → only approximately; transition zone is mixed

rows.concept: Indian municipal water supply (Bangalore BWSSB, Delhi DJB, Mumbai BMC): pipe design uses Re to prevent turbulent losses + pump-power-spec.
rows.notes: parent: reynolds_number_atomic; **public-service-strand anchor: Indian municipal utilities**

rows.concept: T = F/L (force per unit length along surface); equivalently energy per unit area (J/m²). Origin: net inward cohesive force on surface molecules. T_water ≈ 73 × 10⁻³ N/m at 20°C.
rows.notes: FM-G9; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "surface tension floats things" → only when surface tension force >> weight (insect-scale, NOT ship-scale)

rows.concept: Surface tension minimises surface area → sphere for fixed volume. Rain droplets, mercury beads, soap bubbles all approach spherical.
rows.notes: parent: surface_tension_atomic

rows.concept: Water strider (पानी का कीड़ा — observable in Indian ponds) walks on water: weight = surface-tension support across legs. Demonstrates scale-dependence.
rows.notes: parent: surface_tension_atomic; **consumer/observation-strand anchor: Indian ponds**

rows.concept: Inside soap bubble: ΔP = 4T/R (two surfaces). Inside water droplet: ΔP = 2T/R (one surface). Smaller bubble → higher inside pressure.
rows.notes: parent: surface_tension_atomic

rows.concept: Capillary rise in narrow tube: h = 2T cosθ / (ρgr). Angle of contact θ determines rise (θ<90°: rise) or depression (θ>90°: e.g., mercury in glass).
rows.notes: FM-G10; **agriculture + biology bridges**

rows.concept: ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) studies capillary rise in soil — controls irrigation efficiency. Drip irrigation (Jain Irrigation Jalgaon) leverages capillary distribution.
rows.notes: parent: capillary_action_atomic; **agriculture-strand anchor: ICAR + Jain Irrigation**

rows.concept: Plant xylem tubes lift water from roots to leaves via capillary + transpiration. Critical for Indian farming + horticulture.
rows.notes: parent: capillary_action_atomic; **agriculture/biology-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Paper chromatography (Class 11-12 lab + DRDO forensics) uses capillary rise in paper fibres to separate dye components.
rows.notes: parent: capillary_action_atomic; **research/education-strand anchor**

rows.concept: A wave transports energy/disturbance from one region of space to another **without bulk motion of matter**
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.1 (queue analogy + "Hello" example); NCERT §15.1 (pebble-in-pond, cork-pieces-on-water explicit); DCWT §17.1 (energy transfer definition). **v1?:** TRUE — opens the topic. **PRIMARY aha:** the medium doesn't travel; the disturbance does.

rows.concept: Person in queue leans → pushes neighbor → jerk propagates without anyone walking forward
rows.notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV1 §15.1 (the classic Indian-context queue figure). Strong real-world visual.

rows.concept: Drop pebble in pond; cork pieces bob up-down but don't move outward
rows.notes: parent: A1. Sources: NCERT §15.1 explicit.

rows.concept: Raindrop on calm water → expanding circle. Each water particle locally up-down only.
rows.notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV1 §15.1, NCERT §15.1.

rows.concept: Mechanical waves require a medium (sound, string, water); non-mechanical do not (EM, matter waves)
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.1 explicit definition; NCERT §15.1 (trichotomy: mechanical, EM, matter waves); DCWT §17.2 explicit. **v1?:** TRUE. EPIC-C: misconception "all waves need a medium" → confronted (EM waves in vacuum).

rows.concept: Bell-jar experiment: bell rings inaudibly when air pumped out. Sound is a mechanical wave.
rows.notes: parent: A2. Sources: DCWT §17.2 ("Over the moon, sound waves cannot travel"), NCERT §15.1.

rows.concept: Light from stars reaches earth across vacuum at c = 3×10⁸ m/s
rows.notes: parent: A2. Sources: NCERT §15.1 explicit (Eq 15.1 cites c). Cross-topic-ref Topic-40 EM Waves for depth.

rows.concept: Quantum-mechanical waves associated with electrons, used in electron microscopes
rows.notes: parent: A2. Sources: NCERT §15.1 brief paragraph. Cross-topic-ref Topic-48 Dual Nature. ⚠ partial sim — quantum waves don't visualize as classical disturbances.

rows.concept: Wave where medium particles oscillate **perpendicular** to propagation direction (string, water surface, EM)
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.14 (within §15.1-15.5 scope referenced from §15.2); NCERT §15.2 (Fig 15.2 pulse on string, Fig 15.3 harmonic wave); DCWT §17.4 (Fig 17.3 crest-trough). **v1?:** TRUE. Key visual: string up-down vs wave left-right.

rows.concept: Hand snap up-down sends pulse along x; each string element moves in y only
rows.notes: parent: A3. Sources: HCV1 Fig 15.2, NCERT Fig 15.2.

rows.concept: Wave where medium particles oscillate **along** propagation direction (sound in air, compression in slinky)
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.14 (sound waves are longitudinal); NCERT §15.2 (Fig 15.4 piston in pipe, compressions and rarefactions); DCWT §17.4 (Fig 17.4 compressions/rarefactions). **v1?:** TRUE. Key visual: slinky compression travels.

rows.concept: Longitudinal wave = alternating high-density and low-density regions traveling
rows.notes: parent: A4. Sources: NCERT §15.2, DCWT §17.4.

rows.concept: Push-pull piston creates sinusoidal compressions in air → sound wave
rows.notes: parent: A4. Sources: NCERT Fig 15.4 explicit.

rows.concept: Single jerk = wave pulse; continuous oscillation of source = wave train (or wave packet)
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.2 explicit (pulse if source active briefly; wave train if continuous); NCERT §15.2 (Fig 15.2 pulse vs Fig 15.3 harmonic wave); DCWT §17.3 implicit. **v1?:** TRUE. Foundational distinction before introducing the wave equation.

rows.concept: The general form of a wave traveling in +x direction at speed v: y(x,t) = f(t − x/v). The argument MUST be in the combination (t − x/v).
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.2 derivation Eq 15.1 (explicit); DCWT §17.3 (3 conditions for a wave function: PDE form, single-valued, continuous; general solution y = f(ax ± bt)); NCERT §15.3 (Eq 15.2 = sinusoidal special case). **v1?:** TRUE — foundational equation. **PRIMARY aha:** any function whose argument is the combination t−x/v is a wave.

rows.concept: y = f(x−vt) or y = f(t−x/v) for +x propagation; y = f(t+x/v) for −x propagation
rows.notes: parent: A6. Sources: HCV1 §15.2 (Eq 15.1–15.4), DCWT §17.3 explicit.

rows.concept: Wave equation in PDE form (∂²y/∂t² = v² ∂²y/∂x²). Any solution f(x±vt) satisfies it.
rows.notes: parent: A6. Sources: DCWT §17.3 (Condition 1 explicit), HCV1 §15.4 (used implicitly). `granularity_question: candidate_micro` — single-equation insight.

rows.concept: y = (t − x/v)², or y = A exp[-(t−x/v)/T], or y = sin... — all are wave equations. Shape is arbitrary; ARGUMENT is constrained.
rows.notes: parent: A6. Sources: HCV1 §15.2 explicit example; DCWT §17.3. EPIC-C target: students think only sin/cos count as waves.

rows.concept: Three equivalent expressions for wave speed: v = ω/k = λ/T = νλ
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.9 v = λ/T = νλ); NCERT §15.4 (Eq 15.12 explicit triple equality); DCWT §17.4 (v = ω/k, v = fλ). **v1?:** TRUE. The relationship students use constantly.

rows.concept: Wavelength = distance the wave travels in one period
rows.notes: parent: A7. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 explicit derivation.

rows.concept: The canonical sinusoidal traveling wave: y(x,t) = A sin(kx − ωt + φ). A=amplitude, k=wave number, ω=angular freq, φ=phase constant.
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.6 y = A sin ω(t − x/v), also forms 15.10-15.15); NCERT §15.3 (Eq 15.2 standard form); DCWT §17.4 (y = A sin(ωt − kx + φ) with all alternate forms). **v1?:** TRUE — central equation. Special case of A6.

rows.concept: A = max value of y; particles oscillate between +A and −A
rows.notes: parent: A8. Sources: NCERT §15.3.1 explicit.

rows.concept: The entire argument is "the phase"; determines y at any (x,t)
rows.notes: parent: A8. Sources: NCERT §15.3.1 explicit.

rows.concept: y = A sin(kx−ωt) ≡ A sin 2π(x/λ − t/T) ≡ A sin k(x−vt). All same wave.
rows.notes: parent: A8. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.10-15.12 explicit), DCWT §17.4 ("Extra Points to Remember" callout).

rows.concept: φ in y = A sin(kx − ωt + φ) is the **initial phase angle** — sets the wave's lateral shift / starting position at (x=0, t=0). Determined by initial conditions. φ = π/2 makes the wave start at max; φ = π reverses the sine; etc.
rows.notes: parent: A8. Sources: NCERT §15.3.1 explicit ("φ is the phase at x=0 and t=0. Hence φ is called the initial phase angle"); HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.13-15.15 walk through different φ choices); DCWT §17.4. **Collapsed from former A20 atomic** per founder-style decision W-G1 (2026-05-25) — geometrically a lateral shift of the same wave, not a separate physical phenomenon.

rows.concept: Wavelength = minimum spatial distance between two points oscillating in phase
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.8 λ = vT derivation); NCERT §15.3.2 (Eq 15.6 λ = 2π/k); DCWT §17.4 (Fig 17.3 between two crests). **v1?:** TRUE.

rows.concept: Easiest measurement: λ = distance between consecutive crests (or troughs)
rows.notes: parent: A9. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 explicit, NCERT §15.3.2, DCWT §17.4.

rows.concept: Angular wave number k = 2π/λ; unit rad/m. "Number of waves in 2π length."
rows.notes: parent: A9. Sources: HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.3.2 (Eq 15.6), DCWT §17.4 Note. **UNCERTAIN — flagged for founder review:** is `wave_number` a separate atomic or a nano of `wavelength`? They are tightly coupled (one is 2π/the other). Collapsed here as nano per analogous Friction A16→N15.3 decision; founder spot-check welcome.

rows.concept: ω = 2π/T = 2πν. T = period (seconds per cycle), ν = frequency (cycles per second, Hz)
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (ν = 1/T, ω = 2π/T); NCERT §15.3.3 (Eq 15.7 ωT = 2π, Eq 15.8 ν = ω/2π); DCWT §17.4 (ω = 2πf = 2π/T). **v1?:** TRUE. Inherited from SHM but central to wave equation.

rows.concept: T = time for any particle to complete one cycle
rows.notes: parent: A11. Sources: NCERT §15.3.3, Fig 15.7.

rows.concept: ν = 1/T, unit Hz = oscillations per second
rows.notes: parent: A11. Sources: HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.3.3.

rows.concept: Crest = point of max positive displacement (+A); trough = max negative (−A)
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.3.1, DCWT §17.4 Fig 17.3. **v1?:** TRUE — basic terminology, but worth a standalone atomic because students confuse with amplitude.

rows.concept: **Two different velocities exist at the same point.** Wave velocity v = ω/k (constant, depends on medium). Particle velocity v_P = ∂y/∂t = Aω cos(kx−ωt+φ) (varies sinusoidally with time, depends on position+time).
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 explicit ("This velocity is totally different from the wave velocity v"); DCWT §17.5 (very explicit: vP = −v × slope of y-x graph); NCERT — implicit (Eq 15.11 dx/dt = ω/k for fixed phase point). **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL misconception target:** students conflate the two velocities. EPIC-C: STATE_1 shows student saying "wave is moving at 5 m/s, so each particle moves at 5 m/s" → wrong.

rows.concept: v = ω/k is set by medium properties (string tension, mass density). Same value for all particles.
rows.notes: parent: A13. Sources: HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.4.

rows.concept: v_P = Aω cos(...) — changes sign every half-cycle, depends on time and position
rows.notes: parent: A13. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 Eq 15.7, DCWT §17.5.

rows.concept: At y = ±A, cos(phase) = 0 (since sin = ±1), so v_P = 0
rows.notes: parent: A13. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit, HCV1 §15.3.

rows.concept: At y = 0, cos = ±1, so v_P = ±Aω = maximum particle speed
rows.notes: parent: A13. Sources: DCWT §17.5, HCV1 (worked example 2).

rows.concept: Elegant relation: v_P = −v × (∂y/∂x). Particle velocity = −(wave velocity) × (slope of y-x graph at that point)
rows.notes: parent: A13. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit (Eq iv), JEE 2008 example. `granularity_question: candidate_micro`.

rows.concept: Transverse waves can propagate only in media that can sustain shearing strain — solids only. Longitudinal waves propagate in solids, liquids, gases.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.2 explicit; DCWT §17.2 "Extra Points to Remember" (earthquake P-waves longitudinal, S-waves transverse — S-waves don't pass through Earth's liquid outer core). **v1?:** TRUE. Strong EPIC-C: surprising fact that doesn't fit "all media support all waves" assumption.

rows.concept: Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) arrive first everywhere; S-waves (transverse) don't reach the antipode → proof Earth's outer core is liquid
rows.notes: parent: A26. Sources: DCWT §17.2 "Extra Points to Remember" explicit. Spectacular Indian-context anchor: 2001 Gujarat earthquake, 2015 Nepal earthquake — seismograph readings reflect this.

rows.concept: Restating A1 as a misconception-confronting atomic: when a sound wave goes from a speaker to your ear, NO air physically travels — only the disturbance pattern does
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.1 (explicit speaker example: "The air that is near the speaker at the time of uttering a word remains all the time near the speaker"); NCERT §15.1 ("The water mass does not flow outward with the circles, but rather a moving disturbance is created"); DCWT §17.1. **v1?:** TRUE — **CRITICAL EPIC-C atomic.** STATE_1: student says "I hear you, so air moved from your mouth to my ear" → wrong. Visualize: cork bobbing in place on water while ripple expands.

rows.concept: Speed of transverse wave on a stretched string: v = √(T/μ). T = tension, μ = linear mass density (mass/length).
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.4 (full circular-arc derivation, Eq 15.16); NCERT §15.4.1 (dimensional-analysis derivation, Eq 15.14); DCWT §17.6 (full derivation including alternative v = √(T/ρA), Examples 17.10, 17.11). **v1?:** TRUE — central problem-pattern formula.

rows.concept: [T] = MLT⁻², [μ] = ML⁻¹ → [T/μ] = L²T⁻². So v = C·√(T/μ); exact derivation shows C=1.
rows.notes: parent: A14. Sources: NCERT §15.4.1 explicit step-by-step.

rows.concept: Small string element forms arc of radius R, moves with speed v in moving frame; centripetal force T·Δl/R balances tension components; algebra gives v = √(T/μ)
rows.notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1 §15.4 (with figure), DCWT §17.6 (with figure).

rows.concept: μ = m/L. For a uniform string of total mass m and total length L. Units kg/m.
rows.notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1, NCERT, DCWT all explicit.

rows.concept: Pluck a tighter string → wave travels faster → higher pitch (sitar tuning)
rows.notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1 §15.4. Indian-context anchor: sitar/veena tuning.

rows.concept: Thicker (heavier per length) string → slower wave → lower pitch
rows.notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1 §15.4, DCWT §17.6.

rows.concept: The medium determines wave speed. Frequency is set by the source; wavelength adapts via λ = v/ν.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4 explicit ("does not depend on wavelength or frequency of the wave itself"); DCWT §17.6 explicit ("Waves of all frequencies travel in a given medium with same speed"); HCV1 implicit. **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL EPIC-C atomic.** Misconception: "vibrating the string faster makes the wave go faster" → wrong (only changes frequency, wavelength shrinks; v unchanged).

rows.concept: Tune fork at higher ν → string vibrates faster → λ becomes shorter, but v = ν·λ stays constant
rows.notes: parent: A15. Sources: DCWT §17.6 explicit.

rows.concept: When wave crosses from light string to heavy string, v decreases, ν unchanged, λ decreases
rows.notes: parent: A15. Sources: HCV1 §15.8 (Topic-22 boundary — cross-topic). Touch-mention here.

rows.concept: Speed of longitudinal wave in a fluid: v = √(B/ρ). B = bulk modulus, ρ = density.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4.2 (Eq 15.19 with derivation via dimensional analysis); DCWT §17.6 explicit; HCV1 in Ch.16 (out of §15.1-15.5 scope). **v1?:** FALSE — V2. Belongs jointly with Topic-23 Sound Waves; held here as a wave-speed atomic with cross-topic edge to Sound.

rows.concept: Speed of longitudinal wave in a long solid rod: v = √(Y/ρ). Y = Young's modulus.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4.2 (Eq 15.20, Table 15.1 with speeds — steel 5941 m/s, aluminum 6420 m/s); DCWT §17.6 (Eq v = √(Y/ρ)). **v1?:** FALSE — V2.

rows.concept: Newton's formula v = √(P/ρ) ≈ 280 m/s underestimates by ~15%. Laplace correction: process is adiabatic, not isothermal → B_adiabatic = γP → v = √(γP/ρ) = 331 m/s (matches measurement).
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4.2 explicit (Eqs 15.22–15.24 with full Newton-Laplace narrative); DCWT §17.6 explicit. **v1?:** FALSE — V2. Historically important + JEE PYQ-frequent but conceptually advanced.

rows.concept: When two or more waves traverse the same medium simultaneously, the displacement of any element is the algebraic sum of displacements due to each wave. The waves pass through unmodified after overlap.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.5 (FULL treatment, Eq 15.25–15.34, two-pulse figure, phase-difference analysis); HCV1 §15.6 (briefer); DCWT folds into Ch.18 (Topic-22 superposition chapter). **v1?:** TRUE — included per Stage-1 catalog placing NCERT §15.5 in Topic 21. **Caveat:** the deep treatment (standing waves, interference patterns, beats) is Topic 22. This atomic stays at the *principle level* (two pulses → algebraic sum, then waves continue independently).

rows.concept: At each (x,t), y_total = y_1 + y_2 + ... + y_n
rows.notes: parent: A19. Sources: NCERT §15.5 (Eq 15.25), HCV1 §15.6, DCWT (forward ref).

rows.concept: Superposition holds for small-amplitude (linear) waves. Non-linear waves (huge amplitude) don't simply add.
rows.notes: parent: A19. Sources: HCV1 §15.6 explicit ("we shall only be talking about linear waves which obey the superposition principle").

rows.concept: Two opposite-shape pulses momentarily cancel at overlap (y_total = 0 across string), then continue unchanged
rows.notes: parent: A19. Sources: HCV1 §15.6, NCERT Fig 15.9 (both show this dramatically). Strong simulation visual.

rows.concept: v_P(x,t) = ∂y/∂t = Aω cos(kx − ωt + φ)
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.7 explicit with notation note); DCWT §17.5 (full derivation); NCERT — implicit. **v1?:** TRUE. Bridges wave equation to SHM behavior of particles.

rows.concept: ∂/∂t means hold x fixed (look at one particle); d/dt would mean follow a moving point
rows.notes: parent: A21. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 explicit ("while differentiating with respect to t, we should treat x as constant"). Math-tools subtlety students get wrong.

rows.concept: a_P = ∂²y/∂t² = −ω² · y(x,t). Same form as SHM — each particle executes SHM.
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCWT §17.5 explicit (Eq v: aP = −ω²·displacement); HCV1 worked examples (Ch.15 WOE 2 explicit); NCERT — implicit. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (extension of A21).

rows.concept: Two visualizations of the same wave: (i) y-vs-x at fixed time (snapshot — shows wavelength λ); (ii) y-vs-t at fixed position (single particle's SHM — shows period T). Same equation, different "slice".
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCWT §17.5 (DEDICATED full subsection with both graphs explicitly contrasted, JEE PYQ-frequent format); NCERT Figs 15.6 + 15.7 (separated but discussed); HCV1 Fig 15.4 (only spatial snapshot). **v1?:** TRUE — strong pedagogical asset, unique to DCWT format. **PRIMARY aha:** wave = a function of two variables, and you can graph either dimension.

rows.concept: On the y-x snapshot, slope = ∂y/∂x at that point (not dy/dx since y is a 2-variable function)
rows.notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit.

rows.concept: On the y-t graph at fixed x, slope = ∂y/∂t = particle velocity
rows.notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5.

rows.concept: Two particles at distance Δx on the same y-x graph have phase difference φ = (2π/λ)·Δx
rows.notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit.

rows.concept: Same particle at two times Δt has phase difference φ = (2π/T)·Δt
rows.notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit.

rows.concept: Energy per unit volume in a sinusoidal wave: u = ½ρω²A². Comes from SHM energy ½kx² applied to each particle.
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCWT §17.7 explicit derivation; HCV1 §15.5 (related power formula). **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (energy is advanced topic; not in NCERT §15.1-15.5).

rows.concept: Average power = ½ρω²A²·S·v (S = cross-sectional area). Alternative form on string: P_av = 2π²μvA²ν².
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.5 (full derivation, Eq 15.17); DCWT §17.7 (P = uSv). **v1?:** FALSE — V2.

rows.concept: When two or more waves traverse the same medium, the resultant displacement at any point at any time = vector sum of individual displacements. y(x,t) = y₁(x,t) + y₂(x,t) + ... Follows from linearity of wave equation (T19).
rows.notes: SW-G2; **Diamond candidate**; foundation of all wave-interference physics

rows.concept: Two pulses approach + cross: at the moment of overlap, displacements add (constructive if same sign; destructive if opposite). After crossing, each pulse continues unchanged — **waves pass through each other**.
rows.notes: parent; **classroom demo with slinky or rope**

rows.concept: At light↔heavy string junction: reflection coefficient r = (v₂−v₁)/(v₂+v₁); transmission coefficient t = 2v₂/(v₂+v₁). Energy conservation: r²+t²·(v₁/v₂)·(μ₂/μ₁)·...
rows.notes: parent; SW-G8 quantitative form

rows.concept: Two oppositely-travelling waves of equal amplitude/frequency superpose to give: y(x,t) = 2A sin(kx) cos(ωt). **Spatial envelope sin(kx) determines amplitude pattern; time-factor cos(ωt) makes whole pattern oscillate in place. No energy translation.**
rows.notes: SW-G3; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "standing wave doesn't transport energy" mechanism explanation

rows.concept: Standing wave construction: y₁(x,t) = A sin(kx − ωt) [right-moving] + y₂(x,t) = A sin(kx + ωt) [left-moving from reflection] → 2A sin(kx) cos(ωt). Trig identity: sin(A−B) + sin(A+B) = 2 sin(A) cos(B).
rows.notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

rows.concept: At any node, both kinetic + potential energy of string-particle are zero. At antinode, particle KE oscillates max ↔ 0; PE oscillates 0 ↔ max — total energy of segment constant; localised. No power flow across nodes.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: **Nodes:** positions where sin(kx) = 0 → x = 0, λ/2, λ, ... (spacing λ/2). **Antinodes:** sin(kx) = ±1 → x = λ/4, 3λ/4, 5λ/4, ... (also λ/2 spacing). Antinodes between adjacent nodes.
rows.notes: SW-G5; **cognitive_error_target:** "nodes at boundaries always" → only FIXED boundary; FREE boundary has antinode

rows.concept: **Fixed-fixed string** (sitar): node at both ends; allowed wavelengths λ_n = 2L/n. **Open-open pipe** (flute, hollow tube): antinode at both ends; same λ_n = 2L/n but pressure pattern flipped. **Open-closed pipe** (clarinet, single-end closed): node at closed end, antinode at open end; only odd harmonics λ_n = 4L/(2n−1).
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Standing-wave allowed frequencies on fixed-fixed string of length L: f_n = n·v/(2L), n = 1, 2, 3, ... **Harmonics:** f_1 = fundamental; f_2 = 2nd harmonic = 1st overtone; etc. For open-closed pipe: f_n = (2n−1)·v/(4L), only odd harmonics.
rows.notes: SW-G4; **cross-link to T17 SHM coupled-oscillators normal modes**

rows.concept: f_n = n·f_1: n=1 is fundamental (1st harmonic); n=2 is 1st overtone = 2nd harmonic; etc. **Terminology trap:** "nth harmonic" = nth multiple of f_1; "nth overtone" = (n+1)th harmonic. Cognitive scaffold for student.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Sitar/veena/violin: many harmonics simultaneously (tone "timbre"); fundamental gives pitch; relative amplitudes of harmonics determine tonal character (sitar vs violin sound DIFFERENT at same pitch). **ITC Sangeet Research Academy + IIT-Bombay music-cognition** research.
rows.notes: parent; **consumer/culture-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Superposition of two waves of nearly-equal frequency f₁, f₂ at the same point: amplitude envelope oscillates at f_envelope = (f₁−f₂)/2, but intensity oscillates at f_beat = |f₁−f₂|. Audible as "wah-wah" pulsation.
rows.notes: SW-G6; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "beats happen in space" → in TIME at fixed location

rows.concept: Tabla, sitar, violin tuning by ear: master tuner adjusts string until beats with reference frequency slow to zero — at zero beats, frequencies match. **Indian-classical-music universal practice; ITC Sangeet + Doordarshan music broadcasts.**
rows.notes: parent; **consumer/culture-strand anchor: ITC Sangeet Research Academy + Indian classical music**

rows.concept: Piano tuners (rare in India but exists) use beats between adjacent strings to set "equal temperament" — slight frequency offsets give consonant intervals. Same physics as tabla tuning.
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Standing waves on a string driven by electrical tuning fork at one end; other end attached to weight via pulley. Standing-wave pattern forms when f_driver matches one of f_n = n·v/(2L). Classic Indian Class-11/12 physics-lab apparatus.
rows.notes: SW-G7; **education/research-strand anchor: CBSE Class-11/12 + IIT physics labs**

rows.concept: At f_driver = f_n: forced oscillator (string) responds with very large amplitude (theoretically infinite without damping; practically limited by air drag + internal losses). Bridges to T17 SHM driven-damped-oscillator.
rows.notes: parent; cross-link T17

rows.concept: Microwave cavity resonators (used in Indian Space Programme + DRDO radar): same standing-wave physics in 3D box. Cavity length tunes resonant frequency.
rows.notes: parent; **defence/space-strand anchor: ISRO + DRDO microwave cavities**

rows.concept: Two-source superposition at point P: y_P = y₁ + y₂. Path difference Δ → phase difference φ = 2πΔ/λ. **Constructive interference:** Δ = nλ → amplitude = 2A (intensity 4× single-source). **Destructive:** Δ = (n+½)λ → amplitude = 0. **Foundation for T44 wave optics YDSE.**
rows.notes: bridge to T44; SW-G9 cross-link

rows.concept: I(φ) = 4I₀ cos²(φ/2) for two coherent equal-amplitude sources. Pattern oscillates between 0 (destructive) and 4I₀ (constructive). **Already validated in T44 wave optics YDSE.**
rows.notes: parent; cross-link to T44

rows.concept: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave: particles of medium oscillate parallel to direction of energy propagation, creating alternating compressions (high pressure) + rarefactions (low pressure). Requires elastic medium (gas, liquid, solid) — cannot travel through vacuum.
rows.notes: SO-G2; **Diamond candidate**; foundation of all acoustic physics; **cognitive_error_target:** longitudinal-vs-transverse

rows.concept: Compression = particles momentarily bunched → local pressure increase ΔP > 0. Rarefaction = particles momentarily spread → ΔP < 0. Pattern travels at v_sound; individual particles oscillate in-place ±s₀. **Mass NOT transported; only the disturbance.**
rows.notes: parent; **classroom slinky demo**

rows.concept: Bell jar with vacuum pump: ringing bell becomes silent as air is evacuated — proves sound needs medium. Contrast with light (EM wave) which propagates through vacuum. **Indian CBSE Class-11 lab demo.**
rows.notes: parent; cognitive contrast with light

rows.concept: Audible band: ~20 Hz–20 kHz (declines with age). **Ultrasound > 20 kHz** (AIIMS medical imaging, industrial NDT, dog whistles, bat echolocation). **Infrasound < 20 Hz** (elephant communication, IMD earthquake-precursor monitoring, volcano monitoring). Same physics; different perception.
rows.notes: parent; SO-G10; **healthcare + meteorology anchors**

rows.concept: Sound wave can be described two equivalent ways: **Displacement wave** s(x,t) = s₀ sin(kx−ωt); **Pressure wave** p(x,t) = p₀ cos(kx−ωt) = BAks₀ cos(kx−ωt) (where B = bulk modulus). **Key:** pressure leads displacement by π/2 → pressure-antinode coincides with displacement-node.
rows.notes: SO-G3; **cognitive_error_target:** "pressure peaks where displacement peaks" → no, π/2 phase shift

rows.concept: Stationary snapshot: where particles are maximally displaced (compression edge), they are momentarily at rest — local pressure neither max nor min. Where particles cross equilibrium (max velocity), they bunch densely → max pressure. **Visualisation requires double-track animation (displacement + pressure synchronised).**
rows.notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

rows.concept: p₀ = BAks₀ where B = bulk modulus, k = wavenumber, s₀ = displacement amplitude. For air at STP: B ≈ 1.42×10⁵ Pa; typical conversational speech p₀ ≈ 0.01 Pa with s₀ ≈ 10⁻⁸ m — extraordinarily small displacement, measurable pressure.
rows.notes: parent; quantitative scale-setting

rows.concept: Speed of sound in gas: **Newton (1687) — isothermal assumption:** v = √(P/ρ) → predicts 280 m/s at STP (wrong; measured 332 m/s, off by ~16%). **Laplace (1816) — adiabatic correction:** v = √(γP/ρ) where γ = Cp/Cv ≈ 1.40 for air → predicts 332 m/s (matches experiment). Physical reason: sound oscillations are too rapid for heat exchange → adiabatic, not isothermal.
rows.notes: SO-G4; **Diamond candidate**; classic Newton-Laplace historical-derivation arc; cross-link T26 + T27 (thermodynamics back-edge)

rows.concept: From v = √(γRT/M): v ∝ √T (at fixed γ, M). **Indian-classroom-grade rule:** v at temperature T (in K) = v₀·√(T/T₀). At T = 0°C: v = 332 m/s; at T = 30°C (Indian summer): v ≈ 350 m/s. Audible to musicians during humid Indian summers.
rows.notes: parent; **Indian-climate anchor**

rows.concept: Humid air has slightly LOWER mean molar mass (water vapour M=18 < dry-air M=29) → v slightly HIGHER in humid air. ~0.4% increase per 50% RH change. Negligible in classroom, measurable in precision acoustic engineering (CSIR-NPL standards).
rows.notes: parent

rows.concept: Liquids: v = √(B/ρ); water ≈ 1480 m/s. Solids: v = √(Y/ρ); steel ≈ 5960 m/s. **Solids fastest** because Young's modulus >> bulk modulus of gas. Anchor: DRDO submarine SONAR uses water sound-speed; Indian Railways rail-track sound transmission detectable miles away (rail-ear-press folklore).
rows.notes: parent; **defence + transport anchors**; cross-link T18

rows.concept: Air columns inside pipes support standing waves of sound: pressure node ↔ displacement antinode (and vice versa) at boundaries. **Open-open pipe (flute, bansuri):** displacement antinodes at both ends → f_n = nv/(2L), all harmonics. **Open-closed pipe (clarinet, bottle):** displacement node at closed end + antinode at open end → f_n = (2n−1)v/(4L), only odd harmonics. **End-correction:** effective length = L + 0.6r at each open end.
rows.notes: SO-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **applies T22 to air-column geometry**; bridges to Indian classical bansuri/shehnai/conch-shell physics

rows.concept: Indian bamboo bansuri (Hariprasad Chaurasia tradition) is open-open pipe — full harmonic series accessible (the flute "sweetness"). Shehnai is open-closed-conical-bore — only odd harmonics dominate giving distinctive "nasal" timbre. **ITC Sangeet Research Academy formal pedagogy.**
rows.notes: parent; **consumer/culture anchor**

rows.concept: At the open end, the air column extends slightly beyond the physical mouth (because pressure-equalisation extends into open air). End-correction ≈ 0.6r where r = pipe radius. **Indian Class-11/12 physics lab resonance-column experiment with water column directly measures this.**
rows.notes: parent; **education anchor**

rows.concept: Standard CBSE/ISC + IIT physics-lab apparatus: vertical glass tube + adjustable water reservoir; tuning fork held over open top; resonance heard when air-column length = λ/4, 3λ/4, ... Measures v_sound from known fork frequency. Tests Laplace formula directly.
rows.notes: parent; **education/research anchor**

rows.concept: **Intensity** I = power/area = ½ρωv·s₀² = p₀²/(2ρv) [W/m²]. **Decibel scale (logarithmic):** β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) with reference I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² (threshold of human hearing). **Loudness perception is approximately logarithmic** (Weber-Fechner law). Conversational speech ≈ 60 dB; rock concert ≈ 110 dB; pain threshold ≈ 120 dB.
rows.notes: SO-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "double intensity = double dB" → no, double intensity = +3 dB

rows.concept: Each 10 dB = 10× intensity. 60 dB → 70 dB is 10× more intense, perceived as ~2× louder. Common Indian-context: autorickshaw horn at 1 m ≈ 100 dB; Mumbai traffic ambient ≈ 75 dB; library ≈ 30 dB. Cognitive scaffold: log scale matches human sensory perception.
rows.notes: parent; **consumer-culture anchor**

rows.concept: Point source in 3D: I ∝ 1/r² → β decreases by 6 dB per doubling of distance. Why: same energy spreads over surface 4πr². Important for noise-pollution regulation + concert-hall design. **CPCB India noise standards reference this.**
rows.notes: parent; **policy anchor**

rows.concept: **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) India** mandates: residential zones ≤ 55 dB daytime / 45 dB night; commercial 65/55; industrial 75/70; silence-zone (hospitals, schools) 50/40. Enforcement via state pollution-control boards.
rows.notes: parent; **policy anchor: CPCB + state-PCB regulation**

rows.concept: Apparent frequency f' heard by observer differs from source frequency f when source-observer-medium relative motion exists. **General formula:** f' = f·(v ± v_obs)/(v ∓ v_src) where signs depend on directions and v = sound speed in medium. **Asymmetry:** medium is at rest in lab frame — source-moving vs observer-moving give different formulae (not Galilean-equivalent).
rows.notes: SO-G7; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** source-vs-observer asymmetry due to medium frame

rows.concept: Classic anchor: ambulance approaches → f' > f (pitch rises); passes → f' < f (pitch drops). **Indian context:** police siren, ambulance horn, Indian Railways horn-doppler at level crossings — high-pitched-approaching → low-pitched-receding step-change at observer pass-by.
rows.notes: parent; **healthcare/transport consumer anchor**

rows.concept: Why two formulae? Sound wave is carried by medium (air at rest). If source moves toward observer, wavelength in front compresses (λ' < λ); v_sound unchanged → f' = v/λ' > f. If observer moves toward stationary source, λ unchanged but observer sweeps through wavefronts faster → f' = (v+v_obs)/λ > f. **Different physical mechanisms.**
rows.notes: parent; cognitive scaffold; **EM-wave Doppler in T38 is symmetric (relativistic) — important contrast**

rows.concept: When v_src > v_sound (supersonic): wavefronts pile up into a Mach cone of half-angle sin θ = v/v_src. Sonic boom = shockwave reaching observer. **Indian anchor: HAL Tejas LCA + IAF Sukhoi/Rafale supersonic test flights over Pokhran + Bay of Bengal corridor.**
rows.notes: parent; SO-G9; **defence anchor: HAL + IAF**

rows.concept: Police speed-guns + Indian traffic-radar (Delhi/Mumbai/Bengaluru): emit microwave, measure reflected-wave Doppler shift → vehicle speed. ISRO weather-radar (Doppler weather imaging): wind-velocity from rain-droplet reflection shift. **Same Doppler principle — observer + reflector + source roles inverted.**
rows.notes: parent; **transport + meteorology anchors**
