rows.concept: Apply T22 beats to Doppler: two slightly-detuned tuning forks (e.g., 256 Hz vs 258 Hz) produce 2 Hz beats. If one fork moves, Doppler shifts its f, changing beat rate — classic Indian-physics-lab tuning-fork experiment. **Cross-reference T22 beats_atomic.**
rows.notes: parent; SO-G8; cross-link T22

rows.concept: Temperature: measure of thermal energy; thermometric property. Three scales: Celsius (water-ice 0°C, boiling 100°C), Kelvin (absolute, 0 K = −273.15°C), Fahrenheit (legacy). T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15. ΔT(K) = ΔT(°C).
rows.notes: TP-G1; foundational; bridges T27 microscopic-T to macroscopic-T

rows.concept: ΔT in Kelvin = ΔT in Celsius (since both scales have 1-unit = 1-K size). All thermal-physics equations use ΔT directly — Kelvin-vs-Celsius doesn't matter for differences. Critical for student.
rows.notes: parent: temperature_scales_atomic; cognitive scaffold

rows.concept: AIIMS + apollo + Class-12 lab clinical thermometer: 35-42°C range, mercury or digital. Indian-context: fever monitoring during dengue/COVID waves.
rows.notes: parent: temperature_scales_atomic; **healthcare-strand anchor: AIIMS + Apollo**

rows.concept: Solid bodies expand on heating: ΔL = αL₀ΔT (linear); ΔA = βA₀ΔT (area, β ≈ 2α); ΔV = γV₀ΔT (volume, γ ≈ 3α). Coefficient α depends on material. Steel α ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /K; copper ≈ 17 × 10⁻⁶; aluminum ≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶.
rows.notes: TP-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "α/β/γ independent" → β ≈ 2α, γ ≈ 3α from same mechanism

rows.concept: β = 2α; γ = 3α (to leading order). Derivation: differentiate (L+ΔL)² = L² + 2LΔL + (ΔL)² and (L+ΔL)³ similarly. Cognitive scaffold.
rows.notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic

rows.concept: Indian Railways 1 km steel rail expands ~1.2 cm per 10°C ΔT. Expansion gaps (4-6 mm) every 13 m rail section. Welded long-rail uses pre-stressing. **Tata Steel + SAIL spec'd to standard α.**
rows.notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic; **transport/industry-strand anchor: Indian Railways + Tata Steel**

rows.concept: Water has MAXIMUM density at 4°C; expands on cooling 4→0°C. Causes ice to float + Indian lake-fish to survive winter beneath ice cap. Critical to monsoon climate.
rows.notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic; **agriculture/biology-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Liquid γ_water ≈ 207 × 10⁻⁶ /K. Gas: PV = nRT means γ_gas dominated by P-T behaviour (V proportional to T at constant P). **Cross-cluster link to T20 fluid density-T dependence.**
rows.notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic; **cross-cluster bridge to T20**

rows.concept: Heat-lost = Heat-gained in mixing without phase-change: m₁C₁(T₁−T_f) = m₂C₂(T_f−T₂). Conservation of energy applied to thermal interactions.
rows.notes: TP-G3; **Diamond candidate**; mixing-water-temperatures sim

rows.concept: Water equivalent W of calorimeter: heat absorbed by calorimeter = W·C_water·ΔT. Standard Indian physics-lab equipment + IIT-Bombay+IIT-Madras Class-11/12 lab kits.
rows.notes: parent: calorimetry_principle_atomic; **education/research-strand anchor: IIT lab kits**

rows.concept: Q = mCΔT; energy to raise unit mass by unit temperature. Water C ≈ 4186 J/kg·K (exceptionally high); ice ≈ 2100; steam ≈ 2010; iron ≈ 460; copper ≈ 385. **Bridges T26 Cv/Cp gas-specific-heats to operational form.**
rows.notes: TP-G4; **closes T26 forward-edge** (Cv defined → operational Q = mCΔT form). **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed `calorimetry_principle_atomic` from Requires — Q=mCΔT (specific heat) is the fundamental definition; calorimetry (heat-lost = heat-gained) USES it, so calorimetry requires specific-heat, not the reverse.

rows.concept: Water's high C anchors Indian monsoon thermal regulation: oceans heat slowly + cool slowly → moderate coastal climates. Cooking: 1 L water at 25°C → 100°C needs 314 kJ.
rows.notes: parent: specific_heat_solid_liquid_atomic; **meteorology/consumer-strand anchor: monsoon + cooking**

rows.concept: Q = mL; energy absorbed/released at phase transition WITHOUT temperature change. L_f (fusion) ice→water = 334 kJ/kg. L_v (vaporisation) water→steam = 2260 kJ/kg.
rows.notes: TP-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "ice at 0°C = water at 0°C energetically" → no, 334 kJ/kg difference

rows.concept: Standard Indian pressure cooker (Hawkins, Prestige): raises boiling-point to ~120°C at 1.5-2 atm; uses L_v released on condensation to cook food faster + with less fuel.
rows.notes: parent: latent_heat_atomic; **consumer/industry-strand anchor: Hawkins + Prestige**

rows.concept: Human sweating in Indian-summer (40-45°C): L_v ≈ 2400 kJ/kg at 35°C → evaporative cooling. 1 g sweat removes 2.4 kJ from skin. **Healthcare/physiology Indian context.**
rows.notes: parent: latent_heat_atomic; **healthcare-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Fourier's law: Q/t = kA(ΔT/L). k = thermal conductivity. Cu k ≈ 400 W/m·K; Fe ≈ 80; glass ≈ 1; air ≈ 0.025; wool ≈ 0.04 (insulator).
rows.notes: TP-G6

rows.concept: Indian housing materials: brick (k ≈ 0.8) vs concrete (k ≈ 1.4) vs wood (k ≈ 0.13). BIS National Building Code thermal-insulation requirements.
rows.notes: parent: heat_conduction_atomic; **industry/policy-strand anchor: BIS NBC**

rows.concept: Wool sweater + double-layer clothing in North-Indian winter: trapped air (k ≈ 0.025) is the actual insulator, wool just traps. Same principle in thermos flask.
rows.notes: parent: heat_conduction_atomic; **consumer-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Bulk-fluid motion driven by buoyancy + thermal gradient transfers heat. Hot fluid rises (lower ρ); cold fluid sinks; circulation moves heat. **Convection IS buoyancy-driven flow.**
rows.notes: TP-G7; **cross-cluster bridge to T20 — convection IS buoyancy + viscosity**

rows.concept: Land heats faster than ocean (lower C) → hot air rises over land → cool air flows in from sea = day-time sea breeze. Reverse at night = land breeze. **Indian monsoon is large-scale extension of this.**
rows.notes: parent: heat_convection_atomic; **meteorology-strand anchor: IMD + monsoon**

rows.concept: Bajaj + Usha room-heaters: hot-air convection circulates room air. North-Indian winter context.
rows.notes: parent: heat_convection_atomic; **consumer-strand anchor: Bajaj + Usha**

rows.concept: Total power radiated by black/grey body: P = σεAT⁴. σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴. ε = emissivity (1 for blackbody, <1 for grey body). **Bridges to T38 EM Waves** (radiation IS EM).
rows.notes: TP-G8; **Diamond candidate**; **closes T26 entropy ← T-dependence forward; opens T38 EM-spectrum back-bridge**

rows.concept: NTPC Korba + Tata Power Mundra + BHEL boilers operate at 600°C surface temps; radiative loss σ(T⁴ − T_amb⁴) is major heat-loss source; insulation + reflective coatings critical.
rows.notes: parent: stefan_boltzmann_atomic; **industry/energy-strand anchor: NTPC + Tata Power + BHEL**

rows.concept: Sun ≈ blackbody at 5778 K → P/A ≈ σT⁴ ≈ 6 × 10⁷ W/m². Solar constant at Earth ≈ 1361 W/m². ISRO satellites + Indian solar-PV plants use this.
rows.notes: parent: stefan_boltzmann_atomic; **space/energy-strand anchor: ISRO + Adani Solar + Tata Solar**

rows.concept: λ_max · T = b (b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K). Peak wavelength of blackbody spectrum shifts inversely with T. Sun (5778 K): λ_max ≈ 500 nm (yellow-green). Earth (300 K): λ_max ≈ 10 μm (infrared).
rows.notes: TP-G8; **Diamond candidate**; explains why "hot objects glow red then white" + IR-imaging

rows.concept: DRDO + Indian armed forces thermal-imaging night-vision goggles: detect 300-310 K body heat → IR at λ ≈ 9.7 μm. AIIMS COVID screening thermometers use same principle.
rows.notes: parent: wien_displacement_atomic; **defence/healthcare-strand anchor: DRDO + AIIMS**

rows.concept: IMD INSAT satellites carry IR radiometers measuring cloud-top temperatures via Wien's-law peak shift → weather prediction.
rows.notes: parent: wien_displacement_atomic; **meteorology/space-strand anchor: IMD + INSAT**

rows.concept: dT/dt = −k(T − T_env); small-ΔT-from-environment limit of Stefan-Boltzmann. T(t) = T_env + (T₀ − T_env)·e^(−kt).
rows.notes: TP-G9; **cognitive scaffold** to differentiate from Stefan-Boltzmann; **5th cross-cluster use of `calculus_exponential_decay`** (after T34/T44/T48/T35)

rows.concept: Hot tea (60°C → 25°C ambient) cools per Newton's law; characteristic time τ ≈ 1/k ≈ 10-15 min for standard Indian tea-cup.
rows.notes: parent: newtons_law_of_cooling_atomic; **consumer-strand anchor**

rows.concept: Two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third are in equilibrium with each other; temperature is the operational state-variable defining "thermally equilibrated"
rows.notes: TD-G2; foundational; cognitive-prep for "temperature ≠ heat"

rows.concept: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15; T(°F) = (9/5)T(°C) + 32; absolute zero = 0 K = −273.15 °C; Kelvin = SI primary
rows.notes: parent: zeroth_law_atomic

rows.concept: CSIR-NPL Delhi maintains India's primary temperature standard (ITS-90 platinum-resistance thermometer); gas thermometer = primary definition basis
rows.notes: parent: zeroth_law_atomic; **research-strand anchor: CSIR-NPL**

rows.concept: U = sum of kinetic + potential energies of all molecules; STATE function (path-independent); depends only on (T, V, n) for an ideal gas → depends only on T
rows.notes: TD-G4; state-function-vs-path-function is the central conceptual distinction

rows.concept: State function: U, T, P, V, S (depend only on the state, ΔU = U_f − U_i). Path function: Q, W (depend on the path between states). **Cognitive-error-prevention nano**.
rows.notes: parent: internal_energy_atomic; TD-G4 cognitive-error-prevention

rows.concept: ΔU = Q − W; energy conservation generalised to heat; Q heat ADDED to system, W work DONE BY system
rows.notes: TD-G3; **closes anticipated bridge from T13 Work-Energy**

rows.concept: Physics convention (NCERT/HCV/DCP): Q in = +, W by system = +; ΔU = Q − W. Chemistry convention: Q in = +, W on system = +; ΔU = Q + W. Same physics, opposite sign for W. **Cognitive-error-prevention nano — explicit contrast required.**
rows.notes: parent: first_law_atomic; TD-G3 cognitive-error-prevention

rows.concept: W = ∫P dV (area under P-V curve); positive when V increases (expansion), negative when V decreases (compression)
rows.notes: parent: first_law_atomic

rows.concept: T = constant; for ideal gas ΔU = 0 → Q = W; W = nRT ln(V_f/V_i); PV = constant (Boyle's law)
rows.notes: TD-G5; Diamond candidate — PV hyperbola sim

rows.concept: Slow cooling of a car/scooter radiator at idle ≈ isothermal heat rejection at constant T_engine; Maruti Suzuki / Tata Motors / Bajaj engines designed around isothermal idle
rows.notes: parent: isothermal_process_atomic; consumer/transport anchor

rows.concept: Q = 0 (no heat exchange); ΔU = −W; PV^γ = constant; T₁V₁^(γ−1) = T₂V₂^(γ−1); steeper PV-curve than isothermal
rows.notes: TD-G5; Diamond candidate — adiabatic-vs-isothermal overlay sim

rows.concept: Side-by-side PV-overlay: both start at same (P,V), isothermal stays on PV=const, adiabatic on PV^γ=const (steeper, since γ>1). T drops in adiabatic expansion (Q=0 → all work comes from U → U drops → T drops). **Cognitive-error-prevention nano**.
rows.notes: parent: adiabatic_process_atomic; TD-G5 cognitive-error-prevention

rows.concept: Diesel engine compression-stroke is approximately adiabatic; T rises ~5× to auto-ignition temperature ~500 °C — fuel ignites without spark. Indian Railways WDM/WDG diesel locomotives + Tata/Mahindra/Ashok-Leyland diesel trucks operate on this.
rows.notes: parent: adiabatic_process_atomic; transport-strand anchor

rows.concept: P = constant; W = P·ΔV; ΔU = nCv·ΔT; Q = nCp·ΔT
rows.notes: TD-G1; one of four canonical processes

rows.concept: Pressure-cooker pre-vent: isobaric heating at ~2 atm; raises water boiling point to ~120 °C → faster cooking. Hawkins/Prestige household appliance everywhere in India
rows.notes: parent: isobaric_process_atomic; everyday-Indian anchor

rows.concept: V = constant; W = 0; ΔU = Q = nCv·ΔT; pressure scales linearly with T
rows.notes: TD-G1; one of four canonical processes

rows.concept: Petrol engine combustion-stroke is approximately isochoric (volume constant during the brief ignition); used in Maruti/Honda/Bajaj petrol engines + Indian 2-wheeler market (~20M units/yr)
rows.notes: parent: isochoric_process_atomic; consumer-transport anchor

rows.concept: C = Q/(nΔT); Cv (constant volume) and Cp (constant pressure); Cp − Cv = R (Mayer's relation, ideal gas); γ = Cp/Cv (adiabatic index)
rows.notes: TD-G1; foundational bridge to T27 equipartition

rows.concept: Cp − Cv = R (per mole, ideal gas); derived from first law applied to isobaric vs isochoric heating
rows.notes: parent: specific_heat_atomic

rows.concept: Monatomic γ = 5/3 (He, Ne, Ar); diatomic γ = 7/5 (N₂, O₂, H₂); polyatomic γ ≈ 4/3 (CO₂, NH₃, CH₄). Bridges to T27 equipartition theorem (f degrees of freedom → Cv = fR/2)
rows.notes: parent: specific_heat_atomic; cross-cluster bridge to T27

rows.concept: 4-stroke reversible cycle: isothermal-expansion (T_hot) → adiabatic-expansion → isothermal-compression (T_cold) → adiabatic-compression. η_Carnot = 1 − T_cold/T_hot
rows.notes: TD-G6; Diamond candidate — 4-stroke PV+TS sim

rows.concept: η_real ≤ η_Carnot for any heat engine operating between T_hot and T_cold; equality only for reversible engine. Foundation of second-law optimality.
rows.notes: parent: carnot_cycle_atomic

rows.concept: NTPC supercritical coal plants (Sipat, Vindhyachal, Mundra) operate T_hot ~600 °C steam, T_cold ~40 °C cooling water → η_Carnot ~64%, real η ~42-45%. India's ~75% electricity from such cycles.
rows.notes: parent: carnot_cycle_atomic; **power-strand anchor: NTPC**

rows.concept: Cyclic device that converts Q_hot → W + Q_cold; efficiency η = W/Q_hot = 1 − Q_cold/Q_hot. Refrigerator + heat pump = reverse of heat engine (W input pumps heat cold→hot)
rows.notes: TD-G9; bundles engine + refrigerator + heat pump

rows.concept: COP_refrigerator = Q_cold/W; COP_heat_pump = Q_hot/W; COP_HP = COP_R + 1. Voltas/Godrej/LG India refrigerators rated 1-5 BEE stars based on this
rows.notes: parent: heat_engine_atomic; **HVAC + policy strand anchor: BEE star ratings + Voltas/Godrej**

rows.concept: BS-VI norms (since 2020) constrain heat-engine efficiency curves on Indian Diesel/petrol vehicles — η improvements + emission caps. Indian Railways shifting from WDM diesel to electric (75% route-km AC-electrified) reduces aggregate Carnot losses.
rows.notes: parent: heat_engine_atomic; **policy + transport anchor: BS-VI + Indian Railways electrification**

rows.concept: No process exists whose ONLY result is the complete conversion of heat from a single reservoir into work; equivalently, no heat engine has η = 100%
rows.notes: TD-G7; one of two second-law statements

rows.concept: No process exists whose ONLY result is the transfer of heat from a colder body to a hotter body; equivalently, no refrigerator works without external W input
rows.notes: TD-G7; other second-law statement

rows.concept: Proof that Kelvin-Planck violation ⟺ Clausius violation — assume one, construct a composite engine that violates the other. Establishes the two statements are operationally identical.
rows.notes: TD-G7; cognitive-error-prevention atomic — students think KP and Clausius are independent claims

rows.concept: Reversible: infinitely slow, no dissipation, system always in equilibrium; can be reversed by infinitesimal change. Irreversible: real processes (friction, heat conduction across finite ΔT, free expansion, mixing) — direction of time emerges
rows.notes: The chapter's directionality concept

rows.concept: S = ∫dQ_rev/T (operational definition); ΔS_universe ≥ 0 for any real process; entropy is a STATE function (despite Q being path function — Q_rev/T is path-independent for reversible paths)
rows.notes: TD-G8; **Diamond candidate** — most-confused concept in chapter; **NCERT 2023 condensed; HCV/DCP retain**

rows.concept: Σ ΔS_system + ΔS_surroundings ≥ 0 for any real process; equality only for reversible. Statistical interpretation: entropy = k ln(W) (Boltzmann; T27 territory but cross-link here).
rows.notes: parent: entropy_atomic; cross-cluster bridge to T27

rows.concept: NCERT 2023 condensed entropy treatment (removed standalone §12.13); HCV2 Ch.26 + DCWT Ch.21 retain full treatment; state boards (Maharashtra HSC, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka PUC, UP, WB) retain — author at full depth for state-board + JEE-Adv
rows.notes: parent: entropy_atomic; NCERT-divergence note; same pattern as T50

rows.concept: Molecules are point particles + identical mass + random motion + elastic collisions + negligible inter-molecular volume vs container volume + Newtonian mechanics applies
rows.notes: Foundational assumptions atomic; cognitive-prep for "ideal" vs "real" gas

rows.concept: Real molecules have finite size + intermolecular forces (van der Waals); ideal-gas treatment is the high-T low-P limit
rows.notes: parent: kt_assumptions_atomic

rows.concept: PV = nRT (macroscopic); equivalent forms PV = NkT (per-molecule) and P = ρRT/M (density form). Bundles Boyle, Charles, Avogadro.
rows.notes: KT-G2; **bridge atomic to all four T26 process atomics**

rows.concept: Boyle: PV=const at fixed T; Charles: V/T=const at fixed P; Avogadro: V ∝ n at fixed P,T. All three reconciled in PV=nRT.
rows.notes: parent: ideal_gas_equation_atomic

rows.concept: R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K (Boltzmann constant); SI-unit reconciliation
rows.notes: parent: ideal_gas_equation_atomic

rows.concept: PV = (1/3)Nm⟨v²⟩; pressure as time-averaged momentum-flux from molecular collisions on container walls; central derivation of chapter
rows.notes: KT-G3; **central derivation atomic; Diamond candidate** — molecules bouncing in box → pressure on wall sim

rows.concept: Single molecule with v_x hits wall: Δp = 2mv_x; collision rate per molecule = v_x/(2L); pressure contribution per molecule = mv_x²/V
rows.notes: parent: kinetic_theory_pressure_atomic

rows.concept: ⟨v_x²⟩ = ⟨v_y²⟩ = ⟨v_z²⟩ = ⟨v²⟩/3 by isotropy → factor 1/3 in PV = (1/3)Nm⟨v²⟩
rows.notes: parent: kinetic_theory_pressure_atomic

rows.concept: (1/2)m⟨v²⟩ = (3/2)kT — temperature is proportional to per-molecule average translational kinetic energy
rows.notes: The microscopic-to-macroscopic bridge atomic; **closes T26 internal_energy ← T27 microscopic origin**

rows.concept: T relates only to translational KE per (3/2)kT per molecule; rotational + vibrational KE contribute to Cv but not directly to T. **Cognitive-error-prevention nano** — subtle but central misconception.
rows.notes: parent: kinetic_temperature_atomic; KT-G10 cognitive-error-prevention

rows.concept: v_rms = √(3kT/m), v_avg = √(8kT/πm), v_mp = √(2kT/m); inequality v_mp < v_avg < v_rms
rows.notes: KT-G4; cognitive-error-prevention bundling

rows.concept: Side-by-side table: name, formula, numerical-ratio at fixed T (v_mp : v_avg : v_rms ≈ 1.000 : 1.128 : 1.225)
rows.notes: parent: molecular_speed_distribution_atomic; KT-G4 cognitive-error-prevention nano

rows.concept: N₂ at 300 K: v_rms ≈ 517 m/s; O₂ at 300 K: v_rms ≈ 484 m/s; H₂ at 300 K: v_rms ≈ 1928 m/s (~Earth-escape relevant)
rows.notes: parent: molecular_speed_distribution_atomic

rows.concept: f(v) = 4π(m/2πkT)^(3/2) v² exp(−mv²/2kT); probability-density of molecular speeds; peaks at v_mp; broadens with T
rows.notes: KT-G7; **Diamond candidate** — probability-density curve at varying T = one of physics' most compelling sims

rows.concept: At higher T: peak shifts right (higher v_mp), peak height decreases, total area stays = 1; the high-speed tail lengthens
rows.notes: parent: maxwell_boltzmann_distribution_atomic

rows.concept: Reaction rates depend on fraction of molecules with KE > activation energy → exponential tail dominates; basis for Arrhenius equation (chemistry)
rows.notes: parent: maxwell_boltzmann_distribution_atomic; chemistry-bridge nano

rows.concept: Each quadratic degree of freedom (KE or PE) contributes (1/2)kT to average per-molecule energy; total U = (f/2)NkT for f dof per molecule
rows.notes: KT-G5; **closes T26 Cp/Cv ratio derivation**

rows.concept: Side-by-side table: Monatomic (3 trans, f=3, γ=5/3, Cv=3R/2). Diatomic rigid (3 trans + 2 rot, f=5, γ=7/5, Cv=5R/2). Diatomic with vib at high T (f=7, γ=9/7, Cv=7R/2). Polyatomic linear (f=5-7), nonlinear (f=6+). **Cognitive-error-prevention nano** — students conflate the three.
rows.notes: parent: equipartition_theorem_atomic; KT-G10 cognitive-error-prevention

rows.concept: Diatomic at room T: f=5 (3 trans + 2 rot). Above ~1000 K: vibrational modes activated → f=7. Explains why Cv is not strictly constant.
rows.notes: parent: equipartition_theorem_atomic

rows.concept: λ = 1/(√2·n·π·d²); average distance a molecule travels between collisions; bridges molecular-scale to bulk-transport phenomena (viscosity, diffusion, conduction)
rows.notes: KT-G6; applied-physics atomic

rows.concept: Air at STP: λ ≈ 70 nm; n ≈ 2.7 × 10²⁵ /m³; d ≈ 0.37 nm. Validates point-particle assumption (λ ≫ d)
rows.notes: parent: mean_free_path_atomic

rows.concept: MFP increases at high altitude (low n) → diffusion becomes dominant transport mechanism; IMD/NCMRWF weather models use kinetic-theory-derived diffusion coefficients for pollutant transport (CPCB Mumbai/Delhi PM2.5 dispersion)
rows.notes: parent: mean_free_path_atomic; **research/atmospheric anchor: IMD + NCMRWF + CPCB**

rows.concept: Lab vacuum: P=10⁻⁶ torr → λ ~ 50 m ≫ chamber size → molecular-flow regime (vs viscous flow). ISRO satellite-thermal labs + BARC noble-gas separation operate in this regime.
rows.notes: parent: mean_free_path_atomic; ISRO + BARC anchor

rows.concept: S = k ln W where W = number of microstates compatible with macrostate. Boltzmann's gravestone equation. Bridges to T26 entropy_atomic.
rows.notes: parent (cross-cluster): entropy_atomic(T26); **bidirectional bridge nano**

rows.concept: Umbrella per CH-G1: additive (scalar nano) + conserved (β-decay nano) + quantised (q=ne nano). NCERT §1.5 carries all three in one section — keep them unified
rows.notes: Umbrella per CH-G1: additive (scalar nano) + conserved (β-decay nano) + quantised (q=ne nano). NCERT §1.5 carries all three in one section — keep them unified

rows.concept: Algebraic sum like mass; sign matters. 1-state insight
rows.notes: Algebraic sum like mass; sign matters. 1-state insight

rows.concept: β-decay creates e+p with net 0; pair production conserves
rows.notes: β-decay creates e+p with net 0; pair production conserves

rows.concept: Step-size e = 1.6e-19 C; macroscopic μC ≈ 10¹³ steps, so quantisation invisible at lab scale
rows.notes: Step-size e = 1.6e-19 C; macroscopic μC ≈ 10¹³ steps, so quantisation invisible at lab scale

rows.concept: Class-room canonical: comb in dry hair, balloon on wall. Indian-context: monsoon vs winter dryness — winter sparks abundant
rows.notes: Class-room canonical: comb in dry hair, balloon on wall. Indian-context: monsoon vs winter dryness — winter sparks abundant

rows.concept: Glass+silk → glass +ve; ebonite+wool → ebonite −ve. Mnemonic table (5 entries) sufficient at Class 12
rows.notes: Glass+silk → glass +ve; ebonite+wool → ebonite −ve. Mnemonic table (5 entries) sufficient at Class 12

rows.concept: Touch a charged body to a neutral one → charge redistributes. Charge sharing q→q/2 when identical spheres touched
rows.notes: Touch a charged body to a neutral one → charge redistributes. Charge sharing q→q/2 when identical spheres touched

rows.concept: The canonical 5-step NCERT §1.4 process is the visual anchor. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "the rod must touch" — induction does NOT require contact
rows.notes: The canonical 5-step NCERT §1.4 process is the visual anchor. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "the rod must touch" — induction does NOT require contact

rows.concept: The wire-to-earth step is what separates "induction" from "polarisation". Without grounding, induction reverses when rod is removed
rows.notes: The wire-to-earth step is what separates "induction" from "polarisation". Without grounding, induction reverses when rod is removed

rows.concept: Free-electron model. Indian-context: dry wood = insulator (safe), wet wood = partial conductor (NOT safe — earthing-shock context per NCERT §1.3)
rows.notes: Free-electron model. Indian-context: dry wood = insulator (safe), wet wood = partial conductor (NOT safe — earthing-shock context per NCERT §1.3)

rows.concept: Per CH-G5: nano here, atomic in T48. Just the existence + intermediate-ρ fact + "thermal carriers" hint
rows.notes: Per CH-G5: nano here, atomic in T48. Just the existence + intermediate-ρ fact + "thermal carriers" hint

rows.concept: NCERT §1.3 footnote on Indian home wiring (live + neutral + earth). Anchor primitive
rows.notes: NCERT §1.3 footnote on Indian home wiring (live + neutral + earth). Anchor primitive

rows.concept: F = k q₁q₂/r². Sign convention via signed q₁, q₂. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "force depends on net charge sum, not product" — common confusion
rows.notes: F = k q₁q₂/r². Sign convention via signed q₁, q₂. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "force depends on net charge sum, not product" — common confusion

rows.concept: k = 9e9 N·m²/C² in vacuum; permittivity ε₀ = 8.854e-12. Just the constants
rows.notes: k = 9e9 N·m²/C² in vacuum; permittivity ε₀ = 8.854e-12. Just the constants

rows.concept: The "double distance, force becomes 1/4" pattern. Cross-cluster bridge to gravitation (already noted in T16 catalog)
rows.notes: The "double distance, force becomes 1/4" pattern. Cross-cluster bridge to gravitation (already noted in T16 catalog)

rows.concept: Eq.1.3: F⃗₂₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r²₂₁) r̂₂₁. The split per CH-G3 surfaces the scalar→vector pedagogical gap
rows.notes: Eq.1.3: F⃗₂₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r²₂₁) r̂₂₁. The split per CH-G3 surfaces the scalar→vector pedagogical gap

rows.concept: Same sign ⇒ along r̂₂₁; opposite sign ⇒ along −r̂₂₁. The sign-of-product = sign-of-direction insight
rows.notes: Same sign ⇒ along r̂₂₁; opposite sign ⇒ along −r̂₂₁. The sign-of-product = sign-of-direction insight

rows.concept: F⃗₁₂ = −F⃗₂₁. Coulomb's law respects Newton-III — a sanity check pedagogically valuable for confidence
rows.notes: F⃗₁₂ = −F⃗₂₁. Coulomb's law respects Newton-III — a sanity check pedagogically valuable for confidence

rows.concept: NCERT Example 1.4: F_e/F_G ≈ 2.4e39 for e-p. Establishes "atomic-scale dominated by electric force; cosmic-scale dominated by gravity." Cross-cluster bridge to T16
rows.notes: NCERT Example 1.4: F_e/F_G ≈ 2.4e39 for e-p. Establishes "atomic-scale dominated by electric force; cosmic-scale dominated by gravity." Cross-cluster bridge to T16

rows.concept: Per CH-G4: standalone atomic. Force on q due to system = vector sum of pairwise Coulomb forces. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "the presence of q₃ modifies the q₁-q₂ pair force" — NO, pairs are independent
rows.notes: Per CH-G4: standalone atomic. Force on q due to system = vector sum of pairwise Coulomb forces. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "the presence of q₃ modifies the q₁-q₂ pair force" — NO, pairs are independent

rows.concept: NCERT §1.7 application examples (equilateral triangle of 3 charges + 4-charge square problems). Direct superposition usage
rows.notes: NCERT §1.7 application examples (equilateral triangle of 3 charges + 4-charge square problems). Direct superposition usage

rows.concept: E⃗ = F⃗/q for test charge q→0. The "field exists without test charge" conceptual shift. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "no force = no field" (field can exist; force shows up only when test charge present)
rows.notes: E⃗ = F⃗/q for test charge q→0. The "field exists without test charge" conceptual shift. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "no force = no field" (field can exist; force shows up only when test charge present)

rows.concept: Why q→0: large test charge would disturb the source distribution. HCV §29.3 explicit on this
rows.notes: Why q→0: large test charge would disturb the source distribution. HCV §29.3 explicit on this

rows.concept: E⃗ points outward for +Q, inward for −Q. Sphere-of-symmetry insight
rows.notes: E⃗ points outward for +Q, inward for −Q. Sphere-of-symmetry insight

rows.concept: E⃗ = Σ E⃗_i. Direct from superposition. NCERT Eq.1.10. Visual: 3-charge field-vector composition at a point
rows.notes: E⃗ = Σ E⃗_i. Direct from superposition. NCERT Eq.1.10. Visual: 3-charge field-vector composition at a point

rows.concept: The 6 properties (start +, end −, never cross, density ∝ |E|, perpendicular to conductor surface, tangent gives direction). EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "field lines are physical objects" — they're geometric construction
rows.notes: The 6 properties (start +, end −, never cross, density ∝ |E|, perpendicular to conductor surface, tangent gives direction). EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "field lines are physical objects" — they're geometric construction

rows.concept: Because the field has a unique direction at every point. Pedagogically critical because students draw crossings in homework
rows.notes: Because the field has a unique direction at every point. Pedagogically critical because students draw crossings in homework

rows.concept: Bridge to A14: if not ⊥, surface charges redistribute until equilibrium
rows.notes: Bridge to A14: if not ⊥, surface charges redistribute until equilibrium

rows.concept: Per CH-G6: standalone atomic. The redistribution argument (HCV §29.13 + NCERT §1.15). High `Required-by` — foundation for capacitor theory, electrostatic shielding, and circuit-potential equality
rows.notes: Per CH-G6: standalone atomic. The redistribution argument (HCV §29.13 + NCERT §1.15). High `Required-by` — foundation for capacitor theory, electrostatic shielding, and circuit-potential equality

rows.concept: The free-electron drift to surface takes <1 ms (HCV §29.13). Pedagogically interesting — "instantaneous" is approximately true at lab scale
rows.notes: The free-electron drift to surface takes <1 ms (HCV §29.13). Pedagogically interesting — "instantaneous" is approximately true at lab scale

rows.concept: Excess charge on a conductor sits on the outer surface. Bridge to Gauss's law (will be derived rigorously in T30)
rows.notes: Excess charge on a conductor sits on the outer surface. Bridge to Gauss's law (will be derived rigorously in T30)

rows.concept: Two types: positive (proton, glass rod after silk-rub) + negative (electron, plastic rod after fur-rub). Like repels, unlike attracts. **Indian-context anchor:** polyester saree spark in dry weather (NCERT §1.1 first paragraph).
rows.notes: Two types: positive (proton, glass rod after silk-rub) + negative (electron, plastic rod after fur-rub). Like repels, unlike attracts. **Indian-context anchor:** polyester saree spark in dry weather (NCERT §1.1 first paragraph).

rows.concept: Visual: two pith-balls hanging from threads; both repel or both attract depending on charge sign. NCERT Fig 1.1.
rows.notes: Visual: two pith-balls hanging from threads; both repel or both attract depending on charge sign. NCERT Fig 1.1.

rows.concept: Glass-rod-after-silk = +, plastic-rod-after-fur = −. Sign convention by Benjamin Franklin. Historical/conventional, no deep physics. NCERT §1.2.
rows.notes: Glass-rod-after-silk = +, plastic-rod-after-fur = −. Sign convention by Benjamin Franklin. Historical/conventional, no deep physics. NCERT §1.2.

rows.concept: Charge always integer multiple of e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C. Macroscopic charges are huge n. **Per E-G2: separate from conservation.** NCERT §1.5.3; HCV2 §29.1 ("charge is quantized"); DCEM §24.2 #3.
rows.notes: Charge always integer multiple of e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C. Macroscopic charges are huge n. **Per E-G2: separate from conservation.** NCERT §1.5.3; HCV2 §29.1 ("charge is quantized"); DCEM §24.2 #3.

rows.concept: 1 C / 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ ≈ 6.25×10¹⁸ electrons. NCERT Ex 1.2 (200 years to accumulate 1 C at 10⁹ e/s). HCV2 W.Ex 4.
rows.notes: 1 C / 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ ≈ 6.25×10¹⁸ electrons. NCERT Ex 1.2 (200 years to accumulate 1 C at 10⁹ e/s). HCV2 W.Ex 4.

rows.concept: At macroscopic scales the discreteness of e is invisible — we treat charge as continuous. Justifies integration over continuous charge distributions. NCERT §1.5.3 closing paragraph.
rows.notes: At macroscopic scales the discreteness of e is invisible — we treat charge as continuous. Justifies integration over continuous charge distributions. NCERT §1.5.3 closing paragraph.

rows.concept: Total charge of isolated system is constant. Per E-G2: separate from quantisation. NCERT §1.5.2 + DCEM §24.2 #4. **Pair-production / pair-annihilation example** as edge case (HCV2 §29.1: "in a beta decay process, a neutron converts into a proton and a fresh electron — total charge remains zero").
rows.notes: Total charge of isolated system is constant. Per E-G2: separate from quantisation. NCERT §1.5.2 + DCEM §24.2 #4. **Pair-production / pair-annihilation example** as edge case (HCV2 §29.1: "in a beta decay process, a neutron converts into a proton and a fresh electron — total charge remains zero").

rows.concept: Rubbing doesn't create charge — it transfers electrons. NCERT §1.2 final paragraph, HCV2 §29.1.
rows.notes: Rubbing doesn't create charge — it transfers electrons. NCERT §1.2 final paragraph, HCV2 §29.1.

rows.concept: Charges add algebraically with signs: (+1)+(+2)+(−3)+(+4)+(−5) = −1. Distinct from mass (always positive). Per E-G2: own atomic. NCERT §1.5.1.
rows.notes: Charges add algebraically with signs: (+1)+(+2)+(−3)+(+4)+(−5) = −1. Distinct from mass (always positive). Per E-G2: own atomic. NCERT §1.5.1.

rows.concept: Conductors (metals, human body, earth) have free electrons; insulators (glass, plastic, wood) do not; semiconductors (Si, Ge) in between. All three sources agree (NCERT §1.3, DCEM §24.3, HCV2 §29.12).
rows.notes: Conductors (metals, human body, earth) have free electrons; insulators (glass, plastic, wood) do not; semiconductors (Si, Ge) in between. All three sources agree (NCERT §1.3, DCEM §24.3, HCV2 §29.12).

rows.concept: The cloud of weakly-bound outer-shell electrons. Critical for conductor physics. HCV2 §29.12.
rows.notes: The cloud of weakly-bound outer-shell electrons. Critical for conductor physics. HCV2 §29.12.

rows.concept: Per E-G3: one atomic with 4 method-nanos. Conceptual frame: electrons are transferred or redistributed; no charge is created. Covers all three classical charging methods + earthing for safety.
rows.notes: Per E-G3: one atomic with 4 method-nanos. Conceptual frame: electrons are transferred or redistributed; no charge is created. Covers all three classical charging methods + earthing for safety.

rows.concept: Two materials rubbed → electrons flow from donor to acceptor. Glass+silk → glass becomes +, silk becomes −. NCERT 1.2, DCEM 24.4 Charging by Rubbing.
rows.notes: Two materials rubbed → electrons flow from donor to acceptor. Glass+silk → glass becomes +, silk becomes −. NCERT 1.2, DCEM 24.4 Charging by Rubbing.

rows.concept: Charged object touches neutral object → both end with same-sign charge. DCEM 24.4 + Fig 24.1.
rows.notes: Charged object touches neutral object → both end with same-sign charge. DCEM 24.4 + Fig 24.1.

rows.concept: Bring charged rod near conductor + ground → conductor ends with opposite charge. Rod is not consumed. NCERT 1.4 Fig 1.4, DCEM 24.4 Fig 24.2, HCV2 §29.1 last paragraph.
rows.notes: Bring charged rod near conductor + ground → conductor ends with opposite charge. Rod is not consumed. NCERT 1.4 Fig 1.4, DCEM 24.4 Fig 24.2, HCV2 §29.1 last paragraph.

rows.concept: **Indian-context anchor:** Third pin in domestic socket. When fault occurs, charge dumps to earth through the third pin → user does not get shocked. DCEM Ex 24.6 + NCERT §1.3 ("we have to stick to certain pairs").
rows.notes: **Indian-context anchor:** Third pin in domestic socket. When fault occurs, charge dumps to earth through the third pin → user does not get shocked. DCEM Ex 24.6 + NCERT §1.3 ("we have to stick to certain pairs").

rows.concept: F = k\|q₁q₂\|/r² where k = 1/4πε₀ ≈ 9×10⁹ N·m²/C². Force along line joining charges. NCERT 1.6 eq 1.1–1.2, DCEM 24.5, HCV2 29.2 eq 29.1. **Per E-G4: separate scalar atomic from vector form.**
rows.notes: F = k\|q₁q₂\|/r² where k = 1/4πε₀ ≈ 9×10⁹ N·m²/C². Force along line joining charges. NCERT 1.6 eq 1.1–1.2, DCEM 24.5, HCV2 29.2 eq 29.1. **Per E-G4: separate scalar atomic from vector form.**

rows.concept: When a dielectric medium fills the space, F_medium = F_vacuum / K. K is dielectric constant of medium (K=1 vacuum, K≈80 water). DCEM 24.5 #4.
rows.notes: When a dielectric medium fills the space, F_medium = F_vacuum / K. K is dielectric constant of medium (K=1 vacuum, K≈80 water). DCEM 24.5 #4.

rows.concept: k = 8.988×10⁹ N·m²/C² ≈ 9×10⁹ for problems. Derived from c² × 10⁻⁷. NCERT 1.6, DCEM 24.5.
rows.notes: k = 8.988×10⁹ N·m²/C² ≈ 9×10⁹ for problems. Derived from c² × 10⁻⁷. NCERT 1.6, DCEM 24.5.

rows.concept: F₂₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r²₂₁)r̂₂₁ . Sign of charges + direction of r̂ together encode attract vs repel. Per E-G4: separate atomic. NCERT 1.6 eq 1.3 + Fig 1.6, HCV2 §29.2 eq after 29.1.
rows.notes: F₂₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r²₂₁)r̂₂₁ . Sign of charges + direction of r̂ together encode attract vs repel. Per E-G4: separate atomic. NCERT 1.6 eq 1.3 + Fig 1.6, HCV2 §29.2 eq after 29.1.

rows.concept: F₂₁ = −F₁₂. Action-reaction pair. DCEM 24.5 #2. **Cross-topic ref T11 ← T30.**
rows.notes: F₂₁ = −F₁₂. Action-reaction pair. DCEM 24.5 #2. **Cross-topic ref T11 ← T30.**

rows.concept: F on q₁ due to q₂ at positions r₁, r₂: F₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/\|r₁−r₂\|³)(r₁−r₂). 3D vector form for non-collinear charges. DCEM §24.5 Extra Points + Fig 24.5.
rows.notes: F on q₁ due to q₂ at positions r₁, r₂: F₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/\|r₁−r₂\|³)(r₁−r₂). 3D vector form for non-collinear charges. DCEM §24.5 Extra Points + Fig 24.5.

rows.concept: F_e/F_g for two electrons = 4.17×10⁴² (HCV2 §29.1). For e-p = 2.4×10³⁹ (NCERT Ex 1.4). The take-away: electric force is ~10⁴⁰× stronger than gravity. **HCV2 owns this conceptual atomic.**
rows.notes: F_e/F_g for two electrons = 4.17×10⁴² (HCV2 §29.1). For e-p = 2.4×10³⁹ (NCERT Ex 1.4). The take-away: electric force is ~10⁴⁰× stronger than gravity. **HCV2 owns this conceptual atomic.**

rows.concept: F₁ = Σᵢ F₁ᵢ = vector sum of pairwise Coulomb forces. NCERT 1.7 eq 1.4–1.5, DCEM 24.5 #1, HCV2 §29.2 (implicit).
rows.notes: F₁ = Σᵢ F₁ᵢ = vector sum of pairwise Coulomb forces. NCERT 1.7 eq 1.4–1.5, DCEM 24.5 #1, HCV2 §29.2 (implicit).

rows.concept: Two charges on a line; find position of third where net force = 0. Solution exists between like charges (closer to smaller) or outside unlike charges (beyond smaller). HCV2 W.Ex 2, DCEM-style typed problem.
rows.notes: Two charges on a line; find position of third where net force = 0. Solution exists between like charges (closer to smaller) or outside unlike charges (beyond smaller). HCV2 W.Ex 2, DCEM-style typed problem.

rows.concept: Three charges at corners; find force on each. By symmetry resultant on any vertex is along bisector. NCERT Ex 1.6, HCV2 §29 W.Ex 1, DCEM Ex 24.8.
rows.notes: Three charges at corners; find force on each. By symmetry resultant on any vertex is along bisector. NCERT Ex 1.6, HCV2 §29 W.Ex 1, DCEM Ex 24.8.

rows.concept: Charge Q at centroid of equilateral triangle with q,q,q at vertices: net F on Q = 0 by symmetry. NCERT Ex 1.6.
rows.notes: Charge Q at centroid of equilateral triangle with q,q,q at vertices: net F on Q = 0 by symmetry. NCERT Ex 1.6.

rows.concept: Two identical pith balls (mass m, charge q) on threads from common point. Find angle θ where tension + gravity + Coulomb balance. HCV2 W.Ex 22-23, DCEM Ex 24.9 (with dielectric variant). **Indian classroom favorite.**
rows.notes: Two identical pith balls (mass m, charge q) on threads from common point. Find angle θ where tension + gravity + Coulomb balance. HCV2 W.Ex 22-23, DCEM Ex 24.9 (with dielectric variant). **Indian classroom favorite.**

rows.concept: E = lim(q→0) F/q. Vector. SI unit N/C (= V/m). Distinguishes "source" charge Q from "test" charge q. NCERT 1.8 + Eq 1.6–1.9, DCEM 24.6, HCV2 29.3.
rows.notes: E = lim(q→0) F/q. Vector. SI unit N/C (= V/m). Distinguishes "source" charge Q from "test" charge q. NCERT 1.8 + Eq 1.6–1.9, DCEM 24.6, HCV2 29.3.

rows.concept: E depends only on source Q and position r, not on test q. NCERT §1.8 point (ii).
rows.notes: E depends only on source Q and position r, not on test q. NCERT §1.8 point (ii).

rows.concept: E radially outward from +Q, radially inward to −Q. NCERT §1.8 point (iii).
rows.notes: E radially outward from +Q, radially inward to −Q. NCERT §1.8 point (iii).

rows.concept: E = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r²) r̂. Spherical symmetry. NCERT §1.8 eq 1.6, DCEM 24.6, HCV2 29.3. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**
rows.notes: E = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r²) r̂. Spherical symmetry. NCERT §1.8 eq 1.6, DCEM 24.6, HCV2 29.3. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**

rows.concept: E on axis at distance x from ring of radius R, total charge q: E_x = (1/4πε₀)(qx/(x²+R²)^(3/2)). Max at x = R/√2. Zero at center (x=0). Far-field reduces to point charge. **Per E-G5: own atomic.** DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Ring of Charge" Figs 24.12–24.13, HCV2 W.Ex 2.
rows.notes: E on axis at distance x from ring of radius R, total charge q: E_x = (1/4πε₀)(qx/(x²+R²)^(3/2)). Max at x = R/√2. Zero at center (x=0). Far-field reduces to point charge. **Per E-G5: own atomic.** DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Ring of Charge" Figs 24.12–24.13, HCV2 W.Ex 2.

rows.concept: At x = 0 by symmetry forces from opposite ring segments cancel. DCEM §24.6 point (i).
rows.notes: At x = 0 by symmetry forces from opposite ring segments cancel. DCEM §24.6 point (i).

rows.concept: E = λ/(2πε₀r) at perpendicular distance r from infinite line with linear charge density λ. E ∝ 1/r (not 1/r²). DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Line Charge" derivation, HCV2 W.Ex 12. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**
rows.notes: E = λ/(2πε₀r) at perpendicular distance r from infinite line with linear charge density λ. E ∝ 1/r (not 1/r²). DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Line Charge" derivation, HCV2 W.Ex 12. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**

rows.concept: For rod length 2a at perpendicular distance x: E = q/(4πε₀ x√(x²+a²)). Reduces to ∞-line for x ≪ a. DCEM §24.6, HCV2 W.Ex 12.
rows.notes: For rod length 2a at perpendicular distance x: E = q/(4πε₀ x√(x²+a²)). Reduces to ∞-line for x ≪ a. DCEM §24.6, HCV2 W.Ex 12.

rows.concept: E = σ/(2ε₀) perpendicular to infinite uniformly charged sheet. Independent of distance. NCERT §1.15 (in Stage-3 follow-up read). **Per E-G5: own atomic.** Listed here as forward-reference; full derivation belongs to Gauss-law application atomic A29.n2.
rows.notes: E = σ/(2ε₀) perpendicular to infinite uniformly charged sheet. Independent of distance. NCERT §1.15 (in Stage-3 follow-up read). **Per E-G5: own atomic.** Listed here as forward-reference; full derivation belongs to Gauss-law application atomic A29.n2.

rows.concept: Lines tangent to E, density proportional to magnitude. Originate from +, terminate at −, never cross. NCERT §1.11, DCEM (later section, Stage 3), HCV2 §29.4.
rows.notes: Lines tangent to E, density proportional to magnitude. Originate from +, terminate at −, never cross. NCERT §1.11, DCEM (later section, Stage 3), HCV2 §29.4.

rows.concept: At any point E has unique direction → field lines can't cross. HCV2 §29.4.
rows.notes: At any point E has unique direction → field lines can't cross. HCV2 §29.4.

rows.concept: U(r) = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r) with U(∞) = 0. Work done by external agent against Coulomb force. HCV2 §29.5 eq 29.5–29.6. **Cross-topic ref T13 ← T30.**
rows.notes: U(r) = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r) with U(∞) = 0. Work done by external agent against Coulomb force. HCV2 §29.5 eq 29.5–29.6. **Cross-topic ref T13 ← T30.**

rows.concept: U_total = Σ pairs (1/4πε₀)(qᵢqⱼ/rᵢⱼ). HCV2 Ex 29.3 (three 10μC charges at triangle, U = 27 J).
rows.notes: U_total = Σ pairs (1/4πε₀)(qᵢqⱼ/rᵢⱼ). HCV2 Ex 29.3 (three 10μC charges at triangle, U = 27 J).

rows.concept: V_A = U_A/q (PE per unit test charge). Scalar (unlike E which is vector). Unit volt = joule/coulomb. HCV2 §29.6 eq 29.7–29.8, NCERT (later in Stage 3).
rows.notes: V_A = U_A/q (PE per unit test charge). Scalar (unlike E which is vector). Unit volt = joule/coulomb. HCV2 §29.6 eq 29.7–29.8, NCERT (later in Stage 3).

rows.concept: V_B − V_A = (U_B − U_A)/q depends only on endpoints. Conservative-field consequence. HCV2 §29.6.
rows.notes: V_B − V_A = (U_B − U_A)/q depends only on endpoints. Conservative-field consequence. HCV2 §29.6.

rows.concept: V(r) = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r) with V(∞) = 0. Scalar — adds algebraically for multiple charges (no vector decomposition needed). HCV2 §29.7 eq 29.9.
rows.notes: V(r) = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r) with V(∞) = 0. Scalar — adds algebraically for multiple charges (no vector decomposition needed). HCV2 §29.7 eq 29.9.

rows.concept: V_total = Σᵢ Vᵢ. Scalar sum, unlike E which is vector sum. HCV2 §29.7 eq + Ex 29.4 (two charges 10μC + 20μC give V = 27 MV).
rows.notes: V_total = Σᵢ Vᵢ. Scalar sum, unlike E which is vector sum. HCV2 §29.7 eq + Ex 29.4 (two charges 10μC + 20μC give V = 27 MV).

rows.concept: E = −dV/dr along direction of maximum decrease. Components: E_x = −∂V/∂x, etc. The differential link between field and potential. HCV2 §29.8 eq 29.10–29.13. Per E-G7: own atomic.
rows.notes: E = −dV/dr along direction of maximum decrease. Components: E_x = −∂V/∂x, etc. The differential link between field and potential. HCV2 §29.8 eq 29.10–29.13. Per E-G7: own atomic.

rows.concept: E points in direction of fastest V drop. dV/dr is rate of change with position. HCV2 §29.8 "the electric field is along the direction in which the potential decreases at the maximum rate."
rows.notes: E points in direction of fastest V drop. dV/dr is rate of change with position. HCV2 §29.8 "the electric field is along the direction in which the potential decreases at the maximum rate."

rows.concept: Surfaces where V = constant. E always perpendicular to them. No work done moving charge along an equipotential. Per E-G7: own atomic. For point charge → concentric spheres. HCV2 §29.8 Fig 29.11.
rows.notes: Surfaces where V = constant. E always perpendicular to them. No work done moving charge along an equipotential. Per E-G7: own atomic. For point charge → concentric spheres. HCV2 §29.8 Fig 29.11.

rows.concept: V = kQ/r → V = const ⇔ r = const. HCV2 §29.8 Fig 29.11.
rows.notes: V = kQ/r → V = const ⇔ r = const. HCV2 §29.8 Fig 29.11.

rows.concept: If they did, the point of intersection would have two different V values. HCV2 §29 Short Answer Q8.
rows.notes: If they did, the point of intersection would have two different V values. HCV2 §29 Short Answer Q8.

rows.concept: Per E-G6: this atomic absorbs definition + axial field + equatorial field + potential into one cohesive lesson. p = q·d vector from −q to +q. SI unit C·m. Dipole-axis-of-orientation concept. HCV2 §29.9.
rows.notes: Per E-G6: this atomic absorbs definition + axial field + equatorial field + potential into one cohesive lesson. p = q·d vector from −q to +q. SI unit C·m. Dipole-axis-of-orientation concept. HCV2 §29.9.

rows.concept: On dipole axis (end-on, θ=0): E = (1/4πε₀)(2p/r³) parallel to p. Falls as 1/r³ (faster than point charge). HCV2 §29.9 "Special Cases (a)" + eq 29.17.
rows.notes: On dipole axis (end-on, θ=0): E = (1/4πε₀)(2p/r³) parallel to p. Falls as 1/r³ (faster than point charge). HCV2 §29.9 "Special Cases (a)" + eq 29.17.

rows.concept: On perpendicular bisector (broadside, θ=π/2): E = (1/4πε₀)(p/r³) antiparallel to p. Half of axial magnitude. HCV2 §29.9 "Special Cases (b)" + eq 29.17.
rows.notes: On perpendicular bisector (broadside, θ=π/2): E = (1/4πε₀)(p/r³) antiparallel to p. Half of axial magnitude. HCV2 §29.9 "Special Cases (b)" + eq 29.17.

rows.concept: V at angle θ from axis: V = (1/4πε₀)(p cos θ/r²). Zero on equator (θ=π/2). Falls as 1/r² (faster than point charge). HCV2 §29.9 eq 29.16.
rows.notes: V at angle θ from axis: V = (1/4πε₀)(p cos θ/r²). Zero on equator (θ=π/2). Falls as 1/r² (faster than point charge). HCV2 §29.9 eq 29.16.

rows.concept: E_r = (1/4πε₀)(2p cos θ/r³), E_θ = (1/4πε₀)(p sin θ/r³). Total magnitude E = (1/4πε₀)(p/r³)√(3cos²θ+1). HCV2 §29.9 eq 29.17.
rows.notes: E_r = (1/4πε₀)(2p cos θ/r³), E_θ = (1/4πε₀)(p sin θ/r³). Total magnitude E = (1/4πε₀)(p/r³)√(3cos²θ+1). HCV2 §29.9 eq 29.17.

rows.concept: τ = p × E. Magnitude τ = pE sin θ. Restoring direction. HCV2 §29.10 eq 29.19, NCERT §1.10 (Stage 3).
rows.notes: τ = p × E. Magnitude τ = pE sin θ. Restoring direction. HCV2 §29.10 eq 29.19, NCERT §1.10 (Stage 3).

rows.concept: U(θ) = −p·E = −pE cos θ. Minimum (most stable) at θ=0 (aligned with E); maximum (unstable) at θ=π (antiparallel). HCV2 §29.11 eq 29.20.
rows.notes: U(θ) = −p·E = −pE cos θ. Minimum (most stable) at θ=0 (aligned with E); maximum (unstable) at θ=π (antiparallel). HCV2 §29.11 eq 29.20.

rows.concept: For small angular displacement from equilibrium, dipole executes angular SHM with T = 2π√(I/pE) where I is dipole's moment of inertia about center. HCV2 W.Ex 19 (Fig 29-W12): rod with ±q at ends, I = ml²/2, T = 2π√(ml/2qE). **THIS IS THE DIRECT T17 → T30 CROSS-TOPIC BRIDGE.** Uses A21 angular SHM equation from Topic 17.
rows.notes: For small angular displacement from equilibrium, dipole executes angular SHM with T = 2π√(I/pE) where I is dipole's moment of inertia about center. HCV2 W.Ex 19 (Fig 29-W12): rod with ±q at ends, I = ml²/2, T = 2π√(ml/2qE). **THIS IS THE DIRECT T17 → T30 CROSS-TOPIC BRIDGE.** Uses A21 angular SHM equation from Topic 17.

rows.concept: Per E-G1: Gauss's law lives in Topic 30 as one atomic with 3 application-nanos. ∮ E·dA = Q_enc/ε₀. Theoretical formulation from NCERT §1.12 (Stage-3 follow-up), DCEM §24.12 (Stage 3), HCV2 Ch.30 (Stage 3). **Placeholder — full content requires Stage-3 read.**
rows.notes: Per E-G1: Gauss's law lives in Topic 30 as one atomic with 3 application-nanos. ∮ E·dA = Q_enc/ε₀. Theoretical formulation from NCERT §1.12 (Stage-3 follow-up), DCEM §24.12 (Stage 3), HCV2 Ch.30 (Stage 3). **Placeholder — full content requires Stage-3 read.**

rows.concept: Cylindrical Gaussian surface around infinite line → E = λ/(2πε₀r). The Gauss-derived version of A17.
rows.notes: Cylindrical Gaussian surface around infinite line → E = λ/(2πε₀r). The Gauss-derived version of A17.

rows.concept: Pill-box Gaussian surface across sheet → E = σ/2ε₀. The Gauss-derived version of A18.
rows.notes: Pill-box Gaussian surface across sheet → E = σ/2ε₀. The Gauss-derived version of A18.

rows.concept: Inside thin shell: E = 0. Outside: E = kQ/r² (as if all charge at center). The "shell theorem" parallel to gravitation T15.
rows.notes: Inside thin shell: E = 0. Outside: E = kQ/r² (as if all charge at center). The "shell theorem" parallel to gravitation T15.

rows.concept: E_inside = 0 under static conditions. Mobile electrons redistribute on surface until internal field exactly cancels external. **Crucial property.** HCV2 §29.13 + Fig 29.15.
rows.notes: E_inside = 0 under static conditions. Mobile electrons redistribute on surface until internal field exactly cancels external. **Crucial property.** HCV2 §29.13 + Fig 29.15.

rows.concept: All net charge on conductor lives on its surface (volume charge density = 0 inside). HCV2 §29.13.
rows.notes: All net charge on conductor lives on its surface (volume charge density = 0 inside). HCV2 §29.13.

rows.concept: The redistribution is essentially instantaneous (< 1 ms) in good conductors. HCV2 §29.13 closing.
rows.notes: The redistribution is essentially instantaneous (< 1 ms) in good conductors. HCV2 §29.13 closing.

rows.notes: `C = Q/V` for single conductor with V referenced to infinity; depends only on geometry

rows.notes: `C = 4πε₀R`; Earth ≈ 711 μF (NCERT-equivalent example in DCEM §25.1)

rows.notes: "1 F requires 30 km × 30 km plates at 1 mm separation" (NCERT eq. 2.45) — primary anchor for unit-magnitude intuition

rows.notes: C = Q/V where Q is charge on positive plate and V is potential difference; net total charge = 0

rows.notes: j⃗ = σE⃗. Derived from drift: σ = ne²τ/m. The microscopic Ohm's law

rows.concept: V = IR derived from j=σE via R = ρL/A. **EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief (per CE-G2): "V=IR IS Ohm's law" — NO, it's a relation that always holds for V, I, R defined; Ohm's law is the linearity claim that R doesn't depend on V**

rows.concept: ρ(T) = ρ₀[1 + α(T-T₀)]. Conductors: α > 0 (T↑ ⇒ ρ↑ via more collisions). Semiconductors: α < 0 (T↑ ⇒ more carriers). EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "all materials have positive α" — semiconductors and graphite are negative
rows.notes: ρ(T) = ρ₀[1 + α(T-T₀)]. Conductors: α > 0 (T↑ ⇒ ρ↑ via more collisions). Semiconductors: α < 0 (T↑ ⇒ more carriers). EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "all materials have positive α" — semiconductors and graphite are negative
