rows.notes: parent: transformer_atomic

rows.concept: B = μ₀ n I inside long solenoid (cross-link to T36)
rows.notes: Owned by T36; T35 references only

rows.concept: Energy oscillates between L (½LI²) and C (½CV²); ω = 1/√(LC)
rows.notes: Deferred per EI-G6; lives in Tier-2 catalog (AC chapter)

rows.concept: A magnetic field B is a vector field produced by moving charges or currents; exerts force on other moving charges/currents. SI unit Tesla.
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV2 §34.1, §34.2 (full pedagogical derivation from 4 observations of magnetic force); NCERT §4.1 (Oersted's experiment), §4.2.1 (B as vector field, principle of superposition); DCEM §26.1 (implicit). **v1?:** TRUE — foundational. **PRIMARY aha:** B is a vector field around currents, separate from E. **Indian-context anchor:** Earth's magnetic field ≈ 3.6×10⁻⁵ T at surface (NCERT Table 4.1).

rows.concept: 1 T = 10⁴ G. Earth's field ~10⁻⁵ T; lab fields ~1 T; neutron star ~10⁸ T (orders-of-magnitude table)
rows.notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV2 §34.2 explicit; NCERT Table 4.1 (range of B values across universe).

rows.concept: Iron filings around a wire form concentric circles → reveals B-field geometry
rows.notes: parent: A1. Sources: NCERT Fig 4.1(c) explicit. Strong simulation visual.

rows.concept: 1820 Oersted observation: compass needle deflects near current-carrying wire → moving charges create magnetic field. Foundational discovery linking electricity and magnetism.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.1 (full historical narrative + Fig 4.1, Hans Christian Oersted box); HCV2 §34.1 implicit. **v1?:** TRUE — historical anchor. **Indian-context:** This experiment is the gateway from Class 12 electrostatics (Topic 30) into magnetism (Topic 36); JEE syllabus boundary.

rows.concept: Force on a charged particle q moving at velocity v in magnetic field B: **F = q(v × B)**. Magnitude F = qvB sin θ.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.2.2 (Eq 4.3 combined Lorentz F = q[E + v×B]); DCEM §26.2 explicit; HCV2 §34.2 (Eq 34.1 derived from 4 observations). **v1?:** TRUE — central formula. **Already shipped (consolidated)**; refactor backlog applies.

rows.concept: F = qvB sin θ; θ = angle between v and B
rows.notes: parent: A3. Sources: NCERT §4.2.2, HCV2 §34.2(b), DCEM §26.2(i).

rows.concept: Total electromagnetic force: F = q[E + v×B]. When both E and B present.
rows.notes: parent: A3. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.3 explicit. Cross-topic-ref CT4.

rows.concept: Reversing q sign reverses F direction
rows.notes: parent: A3. Sources: NCERT §4.2.2(i), DCEM §26.2(vi)(a), HCV2 §34.2(d).

rows.concept: Direction of F = qv × B given by right-hand rule (or screw rule, or Fleming's left-hand rule for current-carrying wires)
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.2.2 + Fig 4.2 (screw rule explicit); DCEM §26.2 (three methods: vector, Fleming's left-hand, right-hand — most thorough); HCV2 §34.2 (cross-product rule). **v1?:** TRUE — critical procedural skill.

rows.concept: Forefinger=B, central finger=v(+q) or I, thumb=F. For +charge or current direction.
rows.notes: parent: A4. Sources: DCEM §26.2(vi)(b) Fig 26.1. Strong mnemonic for Indian students.

rows.concept: Curl fingers from v toward B; thumb gives F direction (for +q)
rows.notes: parent: A4. Sources: NCERT Fig 4.2, DCEM §26.2(vi)(c).

rows.concept: ⊙ = out of page; ⊗ = into page. Convention for 3D vectors in 2D diagrams.
rows.notes: parent: A4. Sources: NCERT §4.2.2 box explicit; DCEM Fig 26.3. `granularity_question: candidate_micro` — universal convention.

rows.concept: F ⊥ v always → magnetic force never does work → kinetic energy never changes. Only direction changes, never speed.
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCEM §26.2(vii) (explicit derivation W = 0 from F·ds = 0); HCV2 §34.4 explicit; NCERT §4.3 (introductory sentence "no work is done and no change in the magnitude of the velocity"). **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL EPIC-C atomic.** Misconception: "magnetic force is a force, so it must do work on the particle" → wrong. Compare to electric force which DOES do work.

rows.concept: F = qvB sin θ; if v parallel or antiparallel to B, sin θ = 0 → F = 0 → particle moves in straight line
rows.notes: **Sources:** HCV2 §34.2(a) explicit; DCEM §26.3 Case 1 (full); NCERT §4.3 (mentioned). **v1?:** TRUE — special case students need to recognize.

rows.concept: Force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in field B: F = I(L × B); for arbitrary wire dF = I(dl × B), integrate.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.2.3 (Eq 4.4 derivation from sum of forces on charge carriers); DCEM §26.4 (Eq i, explicit derivation); HCV2 §34.5 (Eq 34.6). **v1?:** TRUE — core formula for problems.

rows.concept: Sum F = nAL · q(v_d × B) = (nAv_d q)L × B = I(L × B), since I = nAv_d q
rows.notes: parent: A15. Sources: NCERT §4.2.3 explicit step-by-step; HCV2 §34.5 same.

rows.concept: Differential form: dF = I dl × B; integrate for curved wires
rows.notes: parent: A15. Sources: NCERT Eq below 4.4; DCEM §26.4 (iv) Eq ii.

rows.concept: For any closed current loop (any shape) in uniform B: net force = 0 (since ∮dl = 0 for closed loop). Loop can still experience TORQUE.
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCEM §26.4 Case 2 (explicit derivation F_net = i(∮dl) × B = 0); NCERT §4.10.1 (used in derivation); HCV2 §34.6 (implicit). **v1?:** TRUE — important constraint. EPIC-C target: students assume "loop in field experiences net force" → wrong.

rows.concept: For curved wire ACD in uniform B: F = I(AD × B), where AD is the straight-line vector from start to end
rows.notes: parent: A17. Sources: DCEM §26.4 Case 1 explicit. Useful simplification.

rows.concept: F_net = 0 but τ ≠ 0 in general → loop rotates but doesn't translate
rows.notes: parent: A17. Sources: DCEM §26.4 Case 2 + §26.6 link.

rows.concept: Field magnitude at perpendicular distance r from an infinitely long straight wire carrying current I: B = μ₀I/(2πr)
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.7 (Eq 4.18 via Ampere's law); DCEM §26.8 (via Biot-Savart explicit); HCV2 Ch.35 (covered). **v1?:** TRUE. **Already shipped as `magnetic_field_wire.json` — diamond #1.** Has both derivation paths (Biot-Savart + Ampere).

rows.concept: B has same magnitude on any circle around the wire; direction tangent to circle
rows.notes: parent: A21. Sources: NCERT §4.7(i) explicit.

rows.concept: B ∝ 1/r; halve distance → double field; far from wire B→0
rows.notes: parent: A21. Sources: NCERT §4.7(iii) explicit; DCEM §26.8 (iii) graph B vs d.

rows.concept: Grasp wire with right hand, thumb in current direction; fingers curl in direction of B
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.7(iv) explicit ("Grasp the wire in your right hand with your extended thumb pointing in the direction of the current..."); DCEM §26.8(ii) same; HCV2 Ch.35. **v1?:** TRUE — procedural skill, distinct from A4 (force direction); students confuse the two right-hand rules. **Note:** NCERT footnote at §4.7 explicitly distinguishes "two distinct right-hand rules" (one for B-on-axis-of-loop, one for B-around-straight-wire).

rows.concept: Current loop's magnetic dipole moment: m = NIA (N turns, I current, A area-vector with right-hand-rule direction). Unit A·m².
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.10.1 (Eq 4.28, 4.30); DCEM §26.5 (|M| = NiA, three direction methods explicit); HCV2 §34.6 (μ = iA, then NiA). **v1?:** TRUE — bridges to permanent magnets (Topic 37).

rows.concept: Curl fingers in current direction; thumb gives A-vector direction (and dipole-moment direction)
rows.notes: parent: A29. Sources: NCERT §4.10.1 explicit; HCV2 §34.6; DCEM §26.5 method (ii) Fig 26.19.

rows.concept: Looking at the loop face where current appears clockwise = south pole; opposite face = north pole. m points S→N.
rows.notes: parent: A29. Sources: DCEM §26.5(i) Fig 26.17 explicit.

rows.concept: [m] = A·m²
rows.notes: parent: A29. Sources: NCERT §4.10.1 explicit.

rows.concept: Torque on dipole in uniform B: **τ = m × B**; magnitude τ = mB sin θ; zero at θ=0 (stable equilibrium); max at θ=π/2
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.10.1 (Eq 4.29 derivation via forces on each side); DCEM §26.6 (τ = M×B, full derivation + comparison with electric dipole); HCV2 §34.6 (Eq 34.7 τ = μ×B). **v1?:** TRUE — central application.

rows.concept: When m parallel to B (θ=0): τ=0, stable equilibrium (any rotation creates restoring torque)
rows.notes: parent: A30. Sources: NCERT §4.10.1 (explicit), DCEM §26.6 (i).

rows.concept: When m antiparallel to B (θ=π): τ=0, unstable equilibrium
rows.notes: parent: A30. Sources: NCERT §4.10.1.

rows.concept: At θ=π/2: τ = mB (maximum)
rows.notes: parent: A30. Sources: DCEM §26.6 (i).

rows.concept: Far-field (x >> R): a current loop's B-field is mathematically equivalent to a magnetic dipole's field. **No magnetic monopoles exist** — the current loop IS the elementary magnetic source.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.10.2 (Eq 4.31a, 4.31b — full far-field derivation, B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2m/x³)); DCEM §26.5 (every loop is a dipole). **v1?:** TRUE — bridges Topic 36 → Topic 37 (Magnetism & Matter). **Conceptual depth:** Ampere's hypothesis that all magnetism is due to circulating currents.

rows.concept: On axis: B = (μ₀/4π)(2m/x³). Same form as electric dipole field on axis (with m↔p_e, μ₀↔1/ε₀).
rows.notes: parent: A31. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.31a explicit; DCEM Table 26.1 row 7.

rows.concept: On equatorial plane: B = (μ₀/4π)(m/x³), opposite to m. Half the axial magnitude.
rows.notes: parent: A31. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.31b; DCEM Table 26.1 row 8.

rows.concept: Unlike electric dipole (built of ±q), magnetic dipole is the most elementary unit. No isolated north/south pole observed.
rows.notes: parent: A31. Sources: NCERT §4.10.2 ("magnetic monopoles ... are not known to exist") explicit; DCEM Table 26.1 implicit.

rows.concept: Total electromagnetic force on a charge: F = q[E + v×B]. Generalization of A3 when both fields present.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.2.2 Eq 4.3 (combined form is the starting point); DCEM (implicit); HCV2 (implicit). **v1?:** TRUE — completes the Lorentz force story. **UNCERTAIN — flagged for founder review:** is A34 a separate atomic from A3, or is N3.2 the nano-level expression of this? Founder-style decision: keep as standalone atomic. A3 (purely magnetic) + A34 (combined) are taught as separate scenarios in JEE problems.

rows.concept: When v ⊥ B: charged particle traces a **circle** in the plane perpendicular to B. F = qvB acts as centripetal force.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.3 + Fig 4.5 (full); DCEM §26.3 Case 2 (full with multiple expressions for r); HCV2 §34.4 + Fig 34.4. **v1?:** TRUE — central motion type.

rows.concept: qvB = mv²/r (centripetal balance)
rows.notes: parent: A7. Sources: NCERT, DCEM, HCV2 all explicit.

rows.concept: Radius of circle: r = mv/qB. Equivalently r = p/qB = √(2mK)/qB = √(2mqV)/qB (for K=qV)
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT Eq 4.5; DCEM §26.3 explicit (multiple equivalent forms); HCV2 Eq 34.2. **v1?:** TRUE — central formula, high PYQ frequency.

rows.concept: r ∝ mv = p; larger momentum → larger circle
rows.notes: parent: A8. Sources: DCEM §26.3 (ii).

rows.concept: For same q, B: r ∝ √K (kinetic energy). Used in JEE for proton/deuteron/alpha comparisons.
rows.notes: parent: A8. Sources: DCEM §26.3 + Examples 26.5, 26.6 (JEE 1997, 1988).

rows.concept: If accelerated through potential V: K = qV, so r = √(2mV/q)/B. Used in mass spectrometers.
rows.notes: parent: A8. Sources: DCEM §26.3 Example 26.6 + HCV2 problems.

rows.concept: Time period of circular motion: T = 2πm/qB. Angular frequency ω = qB/m. Frequency f = qB/(2πm).
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT Eq 4.6(a); DCEM §26.3 explicit; HCV2 Eq 34.3, 34.4. **v1?:** TRUE.

rows.concept: T, f, ω are independent of v (and of r). Counterintuitive: faster particle traces larger circle in same time.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.3 explicit ("independent of the velocity or energy"); DCEM §26.3 (ii) explicit; HCV2 §34.4 explicit. **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL EPIC-C atomic.** EPIC-C STATE_1: student says "faster particle takes longer to go around" → wrong. This independence is what makes the cyclotron possible.

rows.concept: If v has both ⊥ and || components to B: || component unchanged, ⊥ component traces circle → resultant trajectory is a **helix**
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.3 + Fig 4.6; DCEM §26.3 Case 3; HCV2 §34.4 + Fig 34.5. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (advanced motion case).

rows.concept: v = v_||·B̂ + v_⊥. Force only acts on v_⊥.
rows.notes: parent: A11. Sources: DCEM §26.3 Case 3 + Fig 26.6.

rows.concept: Aurora Borealis: solar wind particles spiral along Earth's magnetic field lines, collide with atmosphere at poles → green/pink light. **Indian-context shift:** also visible from northern parts of India in major geomagnetic storms.
rows.notes: parent: A11. Sources: NCERT §4.3 "Helical Motion of Charged Particles and Aurora Borealis" callout (full pedagogy + Fig). Real-world anchor.

rows.concept: Pitch (distance per revolution along B): p = v_|| · T = (2πm v_||)/qB = (2πm v cos θ)/qB
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT Eq 4.6(b); DCEM §26.3 Case 3 explicit. **v1?:** FALSE — V2.

rows.concept: Crossed E and B fields perpendicular to v: particle undeflected only when qE = qvB → v = E/B. Selects particles by velocity (used in mass spectrometer, J.J. Thomson's e/m).
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.4.1 full + Fig 4.7. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (specialized device).

rows.concept: Cyclotron: charged particle accelerated by alternating E-field between dees, magnetic field bends path. Resonance condition: ν_applied = ν_cyclotron = qB/(2πm). Exit KE = q²B²R²/(2m).
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.4.2 full (Eqs 4.8, 4.9, 4.10 + Fig 4.8). **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (device-specific). **Indian-context anchor:** NCERT "Accelerators in India" callout — Saha Institute Kolkata Cyclotron (1953, Meghnad Saha), BARC Mumbai, IIT Kanpur Van de Graaff (1963), IUAC Delhi, ITER (India collaborating).

rows.concept: Magnetic field due to current element: dB = (μ₀/4π) · (I dL × r̂)/r². μ₀/4π = 10⁻⁷ T·m/A.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.5 (Eq 4.11 + Fig 4.9); DCEM §26.7 (Eq i + Fig 26.28); HCV2 Ch.35 (not captured but standard). **v1?:** TRUE — central law. **EPIC-C target:** comparison with Coulomb's law for electric field — both 1/r² but Biot-Savart has direction-dependence (sin θ) that Coulomb doesn't. NCERT §4.5 has explicit 4-point comparison table.

rows.concept: Permeability of free space; defines SI ampere
rows.notes: parent: A20. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.11(c) explicit.

rows.concept: |dB| = (μ₀/4π) · I·dL·sin θ / r². Zero along the dL line (sin 0 = 0).
rows.notes: parent: A20. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.11(b); DCEM §26.7 (i).

rows.concept: dB ⊥ plane containing dL and r̂; by right-hand screw rule
rows.notes: parent: A20. Sources: NCERT §4.5; DCEM Fig 26.28.

rows.concept: Both 1/r²; both linear superposition; both long-range. But: source for Coulomb = scalar (q); for Biot-Savart = vector (IdL). And Biot-Savart has sin θ angle-dependence.
rows.notes: parent: A20. Sources: NCERT §4.5 explicit 4-point comparison. Strong cross-topic pedagogy.

rows.concept: On axis of circular current loop of radius R, distance x from center: B = (μ₀ I R²)/[2(x² + R²)^(3/2)]
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.6 (Eq 4.15) + Fig 4.11 full derivation. **v1?:** TRUE.

rows.concept: At center of loop (x = 0): B₀ = μ₀I/(2R). Special case of A23.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT Eq 4.16; standard formula in all sources. **v1?:** TRUE — common JEE problem.

rows.concept: Curl right-hand fingers along current direction; thumb points along B at center (and along loop axis)
rows.notes: parent: A24. Sources: NCERT §4.6 + Fig 4.12 explicit. **Note:** NCERT footnote distinguishes this from A22 (straight-wire rule) — students conflate them.

rows.concept: For N turns: B = μ₀NI/(2R). Linear superposition.
rows.notes: parent: A24. Sources: NCERT Ex 4.7.

rows.concept: For a finite straight wire, field at perpendicular distance d: B = (μ₀I)/(4πd) · (sin α + sin β), where α, β are angles to the ends
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCEM §26.8 explicit derivation. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (specialized case; infinite wire is sufficient for V1).

rows.concept: ∮B · dL = μ₀ I_enclosed. Line integral of B around any closed loop = μ₀ × total current through any surface bounded by that loop.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.7 (Eq 4.17a, 4.17b — full with sign convention right-hand rule); DCEM and HCV2 (covered, not in captured pages). **v1?:** TRUE — foundational law (Maxwell-equation antecedent).

rows.concept: Curl fingers in traversal direction around loop; thumb gives + current direction
rows.notes: parent: A26. Sources: NCERT §4.7 explicit.

rows.concept: When B is tangential and constant on the chosen path: BL = μ₀ I_e (simplified form).
rows.notes: parent: A26. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.17(b).

rows.concept: Inside a wire of radius a with uniform current: B = (μ₀I/2πa²)·r for r < a; B = μ₀I/(2πr) for r > a.
rows.notes: parent: A26. Sources: NCERT Ex 4.8 explicit derivation + Fig 4.16 (graph).

rows.concept: Long solenoid (length >> radius): B = μ₀nI inside (uniform), B ≈ 0 outside. n = turns per unit length.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.8.1 (Eq 4.20) + Figs 4.17, 4.18. **v1?:** TRUE — practical device (MRI, electromagnet).

rows.concept: Inside: B uniform along axis. Outside: B → 0 for ideal long solenoid.
rows.notes: parent: A27. Sources: NCERT §4.8.1 Figs 4.17, 4.18.

rows.concept: n = N/L; SI unit m⁻¹
rows.notes: parent: A27. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.20 derivation.

rows.concept: Toroid (donut-shaped solenoid): B = μ₀NI/(2πr) inside the toroidal coil, 0 elsewhere
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.8.2 (Eqs 4.21, 4.22) + Fig 4.19. **v1?:** FALSE — V2. **Indian-context anchor:** tokamak fusion confinement (NCERT "Magnetic Confinement" callout mentions ITER, India participating).

rows.concept: Two parallel wires carrying currents I_a, I_b separated by distance d: force per unit length f = μ₀ I_a I_b / (2πd). **Parallel currents attract; antiparallel currents repel.**
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.9 + Fig 4.20 (full derivation, Eq 4.23, 4.25). **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL EPIC-C atomic.** Surprising opposite of electrostatics: like charges repel, but like (parallel) currents attract. **Indian-context anchor:** Roget's spiral demonstration (NCERT callout explicit; uses a spring + mercury, 5 A current).

rows.concept: I_a, I_b same direction → attract; opposite direction → repel
rows.notes: parent: A18. Sources: NCERT §4.9 explicit + Newton's 3rd law verification.

rows.concept: f_ba = (μ₀ I_a I_b)/(2πd) per metre length
rows.notes: parent: A18. Sources: NCERT Eq 4.25 explicit.

rows.concept: F_ba = −F_ab for steady currents (parallel conductors). Time-varying currents may violate.
rows.notes: parent: A18. Sources: NCERT footnote at §4.9 explicit.

rows.concept: One ampere = current in each of two parallel wires (1 m apart, vacuum) producing force 2×10⁻⁷ N/m between them. Theoretical definition adopted 1946.
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.9 explicit definition. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (SI-unit historical definition; not a teaching priority for V1). ⚠ partial sim — definitional content harder to simulate compellingly.

rows.concept: Potential energy of dipole in B: U = −m·B = −mB cos θ. Minimum at θ=0 (parallel = stable); max at θ=π.
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCEM §26.6 Table 26.1 explicit; standard formula. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (advanced + thermodynamics-of-magnetization preview).

rows.concept: Electron orbiting a nucleus (Bohr model) constitutes a current loop → has magnetic moment μ_l = −(e/2m_e)L, where L = orbital angular momentum. Bohr magneton μ_B = eħ/(2m_e).
rows.notes: **Sources:** NCERT §4.10.3 (Eqs 4.32, 4.33, 4.34) + Fig 4.23. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (atomic-physics bridge; relevant to Topic 47 Atomic Models + Topic 37 Magnetism & Matter).

rows.concept: For ANY curved wire ACD in uniform B carrying current i: total force F = i(AD × B), where AD is the straight-line vector from start to end.
rows.notes: **Sources:** DCEM §26.4 Case 1 explicit. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (geometric simplification useful in JEE-Adv problems).

rows.concept: m = NIA for a current loop; vector along right-hand-rule normal. Equivalent dipole-moment of bar magnet
rows.notes: **Closes T36 A31 forward-edge** (current loop = elementary dipole bridges to permanent magnets)

rows.concept: Right-hand-rule for m direction from current direction
rows.notes: parent: magnetic_dipole_moment_atomic

rows.concept: Every bar magnet ≡ a finite solenoid carrying bound surface current; m = M·V
rows.notes: MM-G8; Ampère's hypothesis — historical unification frame

rows.concept: Aligned atomic dipoles produce net surface current at magnet's lateral face
rows.notes: parent: bar_magnet_as_solenoid_equivalence

rows.concept: τ = m × B; aligns dipole with external field
rows.notes: Parallels electric-dipole torque (T30 A28) — bridge-edge to T30

rows.concept: Small-angle oscillation T = 2π√(I/mB) — angular SHM
rows.notes: parent: torque_on_magnetic_dipole; bridges T17 SHM analogously to T30 dipole-in-E-field

rows.concept: U = −m·B; minimum when m parallel to B
rows.notes: Parallels electric-dipole PE (T30) — bridge-edge to T30

rows.concept: W = mB(cosθ₁ − cosθ₂)
rows.notes: parent: potential_energy_of_dipole_in_field

rows.concept: B_axial = (μ₀/4π) · (2m/r³) along the dipole axis (r >> magnet length)
rows.notes: Parallels electric axial-dipole field (T30)

rows.concept: B_eq = (μ₀/4π) · (m/r³) on the perpendicular bisector
rows.notes: Parallels electric equatorial-dipole field (T30); axial : equatorial = 2 : 1 ratio is the cross-magnet ↔ cross-charge invariant

rows.concept: ∮B·dA = 0 — no magnetic monopoles; B-field lines are closed loops
rows.notes: MM-G5; one of 4 Maxwell equations — T38 references back to here

rows.concept: Unlike E-field lines which start on +charges, B-field lines have no start/end
rows.notes: parent: gauss_law_for_magnetism; cognitive-error-prevention — contrasts to electric monopoles

rows.concept: Earth's magnetic field ≈ a giant dipole tilted ~11° from geographic axis
rows.notes: MM-G4; unifies declination + dip + B_H

rows.concept: 3 quantities decomposing geomagnetic field at a point: declination D (true-north vs magnetic-north angle), dip θ (down angle from horizontal), B_H = B cos θ
rows.notes: parent: earths_magnetism_atomic; Survey of India declination maps anchor

rows.concept: Magnetic north ≠ geographic north; current location near Ellesmere Island; drifts
rows.notes: parent: earths_magnetism_atomic

rows.concept: B = μ₀(H + M); H = applied auxiliary field; M = magnetisation (dipole moment per unit volume)
rows.notes: MM-G6 cognitive-error-prevention atomic — H, B, M as one triad

rows.concept: H measured in A/m; same units as M; B in tesla
rows.notes: parent: magnetic_intensity_H_and_M

rows.concept: M = χH; μᵣ = 1 + χ; relates material response to applied H
rows.notes: MM-G7; unified χ ↔ μᵣ algebraic identity

rows.concept: χ < 0 (small, ~ −10⁻⁵); induced opposing moment via Lenz-on-orbital-electrons; T-independent
rows.notes: Anchor: bismuth + copper + water diamagnetism; superconductor as perfect diamagnet

rows.concept: Superconductors expel B (χ = −1, perfect diamagnetism); levitates magnets
rows.notes: parent: diamagnetism_atomic; Anchor: TIFR Mumbai superconductivity research

rows.concept: χ > 0 (small, ~ +10⁻⁵); permanent atomic moments align weakly with B; χ ∝ 1/T (Curie's law)
rows.notes: Anchor: aluminium + oxygen (liquid O₂ paramagnetism demo)

rows.concept: χ = C/T (Curie constant divided by absolute temperature)
rows.notes: parent: paramagnetism_atomic

rows.concept: Tabulate: dia (χ < 0, very small, T-independent), para (χ > 0, small, χ ∝ 1/T), ferro (χ >> 0, T < T_Curie). Cognitive-error-prevention.
rows.notes: parent: paramagnetism_atomic; MM-G10 cognitive-error-prevention nano

rows.concept: χ >> 0 (10² to 10⁶); domain structure with spontaneous alignment within domain; Curie temperature T_C above which → paramagnetic
rows.notes: Anchor: iron, cobalt, nickel; Indian Rare Earths Ltd Nd-Fe-B production

rows.concept: Below T_C, atomic moments align spontaneously within ~μm-sized domains; applied B aligns domains, not individual spins
rows.notes: parent: ferromagnetism_atomic; TIFR + IIT-B domain-imaging anchor

rows.concept: Above T_C ferromagnet becomes paramagnet (Fe: 770°C, Ni: 358°C, Co: 1115°C)
rows.notes: parent: ferromagnetism_atomic

rows.concept: B-H loop for ferromagnets: retentivity B_r (B at H=0), coercivity H_c (H to bring B=0), loop-area = energy dissipated per cycle per volume
rows.notes: MM-G3; **closes EI-G6 from T35** (hysteresis loss + eddy currents → ferromagnetic loss model); **bridges to T39 transformer core selection**

rows.concept: Soft (low H_c, low B_r, small loop area → transformer cores) vs Hard (high H_c, high B_r, large loop → permanent magnets)
rows.notes: parent: hysteresis_loop_atomic; **bridge to T39 transformer-core-selection**

rows.concept: Nd-Fe-B + Sm-Co + Alnico — high coercivity. Indian Rare Earths Ltd (Kollam, Kerala) + DRDO BLDC motors
rows.notes: parent: hysteresis_loop_atomic; **VERY-STRONG anchor candidate via Indian Rare Earths Ltd**

rows.concept: Electromagnet = current loop around soft-iron core (low H_c → reversible); permanent = hard-magnet shaped + magnetised
rows.notes: parent: hysteresis_loop_atomic

rows.concept: i_d = ε₀ (dΦ_E/dt) — Maxwell's correction to Ampère's law; closes the loop for charging capacitor
rows.notes: Capacitor-charging diagram — i_c through wire, i_d through gap

rows.concept: Ampère loop straddles capacitor plate vs gap — i_d resolves the paradox
rows.notes: parent: displacement_current

rows.concept: Four equations unifying E + B: Gauss (E), Gauss (B), Faraday, Ampère-Maxwell
rows.notes: Qualitative-level atomic (EW-G2); historical-importance frame

rows.concept: Maxwell unified electricity, magnetism, AND optics — light is an EM phenomenon
rows.notes: parent: maxwell_equations_summary

rows.concept: Self-propagating wave of mutually-inducing E and B fields; propagates at c in vacuum
rows.requiredBy: [transverse_nature_em_wave, speed_of_light_derivation, em_spectrum_atomic, ground_wave_propagation(T50 back), sky_wave_propagation(T50 back), space_wave_propagation(T50 back)]
rows.notes: **Bridge atomic — closes 3 forward edges from T50** (EW-G6)

rows.concept: E ⊥ B ⊥ k̂; right-hand-rule for direction
rows.notes: parent: em_wave_basics

rows.concept: E₀/B₀ = c at every point in the wave
rows.notes: parent: em_wave_basics

rows.concept: E and B oscillate perpendicular to propagation direction (and to each other)
rows.notes: Bridges to T44 polarisation

rows.concept: (1) speed = c, (2) transverse, (3) self-propagating E↔B, (4) carries momentum + energy — EW-G8
rows.notes: parent: transverse_nature_em_wave; cognitive-error-prevention atomic

rows.concept: c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀); pure-constants result from Maxwell's equations
rows.notes: Diamond candidate — historical impact + clean derivation

rows.concept: c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s; Indian astronomers verified historically
rows.notes: parent: speed_of_light_derivation

rows.concept: u = ½ε₀E² + B²/(2μ₀); average u_E = u_B (equipartition between E and B)
rows.notes: **Bridges T35+T31 to T38** — uses both energy-density formulae

rows.concept: I = c·u_avg = ½ε₀cE₀² = poynting vector magnitude (time-averaged)
rows.notes: Uses math-tools time-averaging — first-use validation in E&M context (Stage-3 hit)

rows.concept: EM wave carries momentum; P = I/c (absorbed) or 2I/c (reflected)
rows.notes: Anchor: solar sail concept; Indian Aditya-L1 solar mission (partial relevance)

rows.concept: Continuous frequency/wavelength axis spanning 10⁰ – 10²⁴ Hz; 7 named bands
rows.notes: THE iconic Class-12 diagram; each band a nano

rows.concept: f < 10⁹ Hz; λ > 0.3 m; antenna sources; AM/FM/TV/cellular
rows.notes: parent: em_spectrum_atomic; Indian-anchor AIR + Doordarshan

rows.concept: f 10⁹–10¹² Hz; λ 1 mm – 30 cm; klystron/magnetron sources; radar + 5G + ovens
rows.notes: parent: em_spectrum_atomic; Indian-anchor IMD Doppler radar + Reliance Jio 5G n78 band

rows.concept: f 10¹²–10¹⁴ Hz; thermal radiation; remote controls; greenhouse
rows.notes: parent: em_spectrum_atomic; ISRO Cartosat thermal-IR sensors

rows.concept: f 4-7.5 × 10¹⁴ Hz; λ 400-700 nm; ROYGBIV; only band our eyes detect
rows.notes: parent: em_spectrum_atomic

rows.concept: f 10¹⁵–10¹⁶ Hz; sterilisation, fluorescence; ozone layer blocks most
rows.notes: parent: em_spectrum_atomic

rows.concept: f 10¹⁶–10¹⁹ Hz; Coolidge tube source; AIIMS imaging
rows.notes: parent: em_spectrum_atomic; back-link to T45 x-ray cluster

rows.concept: f > 10¹⁹ Hz; nuclear decay source; Bhabha Atomic gamma-knife
rows.notes: parent: em_spectrum_atomic; back-link to T48 γ-decay

rows.concept: Spark-gap oscillator + resonant receiver → first experimental confirmation of EM waves (1887)
rows.notes: Historical anchor; Diamond candidate for narrative-rich sim

rows.concept: LC tuning in receiver matches transmitter frequency
rows.notes: parent: hertz_experiment_atomic

rows.concept: v(t) = V₀ sin ωt; V_rms = V₀/√2; ω = 2πf; T = 1/f
rows.notes: AC-G2; foundational atomic for entire chapter

rows.concept: V_rms = V₀/√2; V_avg over half-cycle = 2V₀/π; over full = 0. Derivation via ⟨sin²ωt⟩ = ½
rows.notes: parent: ac_voltage_source_atomic; **3rd cross-cluster use of `time_averaging_cos_squared`**

rows.concept: India: 50 Hz; US: 60 Hz; BIS IS 12360 standard
rows.notes: parent: ac_voltage_source_atomic; Indian standards anchor

rows.concept: Sinusoidal quantity ↔ rotating vector in complex plane; phase angle = rotation angle; magnitude = peak value
rows.notes: AC-G3; the cognitive tool used everywhere downstream

rows.concept: Inductor: V leads I (V must rise to drive change in I against L); Capacitor: I leads V (I delivers charge before V builds across plates)
rows.notes: parent: phasor_representation_atomic; AC-G10 cognitive-error-prevention nano

rows.concept: Z² = R² + (X_L − X_C)²; tan φ = (X_L − X_C)/R
rows.notes: parent: phasor_representation_atomic

rows.concept: V and I in phase; I = V/R same as DC; P_avg = V_rms²/R
rows.notes: The simplest AC circuit — phase φ = 0

rows.concept: V leads I by π/2; X_L = ωL (inductive reactance); no power dissipation
rows.notes: X_L grows linearly with ω

rows.concept: X_L = ωL has units of ohms; X_L(50Hz) for 1H coil = 314 Ω
rows.notes: parent: ac_inductor_circuit

rows.concept: I leads V by π/2; X_C = 1/(ωC) (capacitive reactance); no power dissipation
rows.notes: X_C decreases with ω — opposite frequency-dependence to X_L

rows.concept: X_C = 1/(ωC) has units of ohms; X_C(50Hz) for 1μF cap = 3.18 kΩ
rows.notes: parent: ac_capacitor_circuit

rows.concept: Impedance Z = √(R² + (X_L − X_C)²); phase φ = arctan((X_L − X_C)/R); I = V₀/Z
rows.notes: Combines all 3 previous atomics via vector addition of voltage phasors. **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed `impedance_triangle_nano` from Requires — a nano cannot precede its parent atomic; the triangle is DERIVED from the LCR result (now in Required-by).

rows.concept: At ω₀ = 1/√(LC), X_L = X_C → Z minimized = R → I maximized; sharpness Q = ω₀L/R
rows.notes: AC-G5; Diamond candidate — resonance curve + Q-sharpness sim

rows.concept: Q = ω₀L/R = (1/R)·√(L/C); also Q = ω₀/Δω (bandwidth ratio)
rows.notes: parent: resonance_in_lcr_atomic

rows.concept: Δω = R/L = ω₀/Q; half-power frequencies bracket resonance
rows.notes: parent: resonance_in_lcr_atomic

rows.concept: LC tuning in radio receiver selects one station from EM spectrum by matching ω₀
rows.notes: parent: resonance_in_lcr_atomic; bridge to T38 Hertz receiver

rows.concept: P_avg = V_rms·I_rms·cos φ (real); S = V_rms·I_rms (apparent); Q_react = V_rms·I_rms·sin φ (reactive); cos φ = power factor
rows.notes: AC-G6; cognitive-error-prevention atomic

rows.concept: Industrial loads add capacitor banks to push cos φ → 1; reduces I_rms for same P_avg → lowers I²R losses in transmission. CEA tariff penalises low cos φ
rows.notes: parent: power_in_ac_circuit_atomic; **policy-strand anchor: CEA tariff orders**

rows.concept: Side-by-side table: name, formula, units (W vs VA vs VAR), physical meaning
rows.notes: parent: power_in_ac_circuit_atomic; AC-G6 cognitive-error-prevention nano

rows.concept: Energy oscillates between L (½LI²) and C (½CV²) at ω = 1/√(LC); analogous to SHM with q ↔ x
rows.notes: AC-G4; **closes EI-G6 deferral from T35**; **bridges to T17 SHM** (most-cited cross-cluster analogy)

rows.concept: d²q/dt² = −q/(LC); compare to d²x/dt² = −ω²x with ω² = 1/LC
rows.notes: parent: lc_oscillations_atomic

rows.concept: At t=0 all energy in C; at quarter-period all in L; full period returns; ½LI₀² = ½CV₀²
rows.notes: parent: lc_oscillations_atomic

rows.concept: **AC-grid extension of `transformer_atomic` (T35)** — the core V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p turn-ratio principle is owned by T35; THIS row adds only the AC-specific physics: soft-iron core minimises hysteresis loss, lamination cuts eddy losses, and step-up/step-down in the 50 Hz grid context.
rows.notes: AC-G7; **closes T37 hysteresis forward-edge**. **De-dup 2026-06-13:** was a near-duplicate of T35 `transformer_atomic`; re-scoped to the AC extension (depends on T35, no longer restates the turn-ratio principle). Kept as a thin atomic — NOT demoted to nano because it owns 3 child nanos (losses / step-up-down / traction); demotion would create nano-under-nano.

rows.concept: 4 loss mechanisms: copper (I²R), hysteresis (T37 area-of-B-H-loop), eddy currents (T35 → laminated core), flux leakage. Efficiency η typically 96-99%
rows.notes: parent: transformer_in_ac_atomic; **closes T37 + T35 simultaneously**

rows.concept: N_s > N_p → step-up (high-voltage transmission); N_s < N_p → step-down (consumer end). India: 11 kV / 33 kV / 132 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV / 765 kV grid
rows.notes: parent: transformer_in_ac_atomic; PowerGrid Corp anchor

rows.concept: Indian Railways: 25 kV / 50 Hz overhead → on-board step-down + rectifier + traction motor. ~70% of route-km electrified
rows.notes: parent: transformer_in_ac_atomic; Indian Railways anchor (VERY-STRONG strand)

rows.concept: Purely inductive or purely capacitive circuit → cos φ = 0 → P_avg = 0 although I_rms ≠ 0
rows.notes: AC-G8; conceptual atomic — "current that does no work"

rows.concept: Inductor used to limit AC current without dissipating power; replaces resistor in fluorescent tube ballast
rows.notes: AC-G8; engineering atomic — practical wattless device. **Bridge edge to T37 ferromagnetic core selection**

rows.concept: Fluorescent tube ballast = choke coil in series with tube; limits current after ionisation
rows.notes: parent: choke_coil_atomic; everyday-Indian anchor

rows.concept: i=r + normal-incidence + coplanarity. Atomic per RO-G1. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "angle of incidence is measured from the mirror surface, not the normal" — universal Class 12 error
rows.notes: i=r + normal-incidence + coplanarity. Atomic per RO-G1. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "angle of incidence is measured from the mirror surface, not the normal" — universal Class 12 error

rows.concept: Normal at any point on a sphere = the radius (line from C through point). Bridge to A4 spherical-mirror geometry
rows.notes: Normal at any point on a sphere = the radius (line from C through point). Bridge to A4 spherical-mirror geometry

rows.concept: All three lie in the plane of incidence. Pedagogically subtle — students draw 3D crossings
rows.notes: All three lie in the plane of incidence. Pedagogically subtle — students draw 3D crossings

rows.concept: v = -u (image distance = object distance on opposite side); lateral inversion; image is virtual, erect, same size. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "image is on the mirror surface" — students locate image AT the glass
rows.notes: v = -u (image distance = object distance on opposite side); lateral inversion; image is virtual, erect, same size. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "image is on the mirror surface" — students locate image AT the glass

rows.concept: Right-hand becomes left-hand. Ambulance "AMBULANCE" mirror-inversion painted text is the Indian-context anchor
rows.notes: Right-hand becomes left-hand. Ambulance "AMBULANCE" mirror-inversion painted text is the Indian-context anchor

rows.concept: h_min = H/2 for full-body view. NCERT Exercise + DCP Q.8 canonical. Indian-context: bathroom mirror sizing
rows.notes: h_min = H/2 for full-body view. NCERT Exercise + DCP Q.8 canonical. Indian-context: bathroom mirror sizing

rows.concept: N = 360°/θ - 1 (or 360°/θ if asymmetric). Indian-context: kaleidoscope toy + 2-mirror parallel barber-shop infinite reflection
rows.notes: N = 360°/θ - 1 (or 360°/θ if asymmetric). Indian-context: kaleidoscope toy + 2-mirror parallel barber-shop infinite reflection

rows.concept: When mirror rotates by θ, reflected ray rotates by 2θ. Classroom galvanometer-scale demonstration; JEE-tested annually
rows.notes: When mirror rotates by θ, reflected ray rotates by 2θ. Classroom galvanometer-scale demonstration; JEE-tested annually

rows.concept: Pole P, centre of curvature C, radius of curvature R, principal axis, aperture, focal plane. Foundation atomic for all mirror-related math. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "the pole is the centre of curvature" — common confusion
rows.notes: Pole P, centre of curvature C, radius of curvature R, principal axis, aperture, focal plane. Foundation atomic for all mirror-related math. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "the pole is the centre of curvature" — common confusion

rows.concept: Concave: silvered on outside, reflects on inside (caves inward toward C). Convex: silvered on inside, reflects on outside (bulges toward observer). Indian-context: shaving (concave) vs scooter rear-view (convex)
rows.notes: Concave: silvered on outside, reflects on inside (caves inward toward C). Convex: silvered on inside, reflects on outside (bulges toward observer). Indian-context: shaving (concave) vs scooter rear-view (convex)

rows.concept: Bridge to A11 spherical aberration: if aperture is large, paraxial approximation fails
rows.notes: Bridge to A11 spherical aberration: if aperture is large, paraxial approximation fails

rows.concept: NCERT §9.2.2 derivation via Fig.9.4 geometry. For paraxial rays, f = R/2. The "1/2" surprise — students expect f = R or f = 2R
rows.notes: NCERT §9.2.2 derivation via Fig.9.4 geometry. For paraxial rays, f = R/2. The "1/2" surprise — students expect f = R or f = 2R

rows.concept: Parallel rays converge to a real F in front of the mirror. Sign: f negative per Cartesian convention
rows.notes: Parallel rays converge to a real F in front of the mirror. Sign: f negative per Cartesian convention

rows.concept: Parallel rays appear to diverge from a virtual F behind the mirror. Sign: f positive
rows.notes: Parallel rays appear to diverge from a virtual F behind the mirror. Sign: f positive

rows.concept: Per RO-G2: own atomic. Pole as origin; incident-light direction positive; heights upward positive. The sign table for the 6 image-formation cases. Most common failure point in mirror-formula problems
rows.notes: Per RO-G2: own atomic. Pole as origin; incident-light direction positive; heights upward positive. The sign table for the 6 image-formation cases. Most common failure point in mirror-formula problems

rows.concept: If light travels left-to-right, +x is rightward (and distances toward mirror are positive). If light is reversed (multi-element systems), sign convention can flip mid-problem
rows.notes: If light travels left-to-right, +x is rightward (and distances toward mirror are positive). If light is reversed (multi-element systems), sign convention can flip mid-problem

rows.concept: For concave mirror with real object: v negative (image on the same side as object, opposite to incident-light direction beyond pole)
rows.notes: For concave mirror with real object: v negative (image on the same side as object, opposite to incident-light direction beyond pole)

rows.concept: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. NCERT derivation via similar triangles A'B'F and ABP. HCV derivation via exterior angle. Per RO-G3: distinct atomic from lens equation despite similar form
rows.notes: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. NCERT derivation via similar triangles A'B'F and ABP. HCV derivation via exterior angle. Per RO-G3: distinct atomic from lens equation despite similar form

rows.concept: The MPF ~ A'B'F similarity is the key insight. Pedagogically rigorous
rows.notes: The MPF ~ A'B'F similarity is the key insight. Pedagogically rigorous

rows.concept: m = h'/h = -v/u. Negative m ⇒ inverted (real image); positive m ⇒ erect (virtual image). Per RO-G3 split: lens magnification is m = +v/u (sign convention difference) — common confusion
rows.notes: m = h'/h = -v/u. Negative m ⇒ inverted (real image); positive m ⇒ erect (virtual image). Per RO-G3 split: lens magnification is m = +v/u (sign convention difference) — common confusion

rows.concept: Negative m means inverted; positive m means erect. Magnitude tells size
rows.notes: Negative m means inverted; positive m means erect. Magnitude tells size

rows.concept: Per RO-G4: 6-state atomic. Cases: (i) ∞ → F (real, dim, inv); (ii) beyond C → between F-C (real, dim, inv); (iii) at C → at C (real, same-size, inv); (iv) between C-F → beyond C (real, mag, inv); (v) at F → ∞; (vi) between P-F → virtual, behind mirror, erect, mag. Plus +1 convex case (A9). The CBSE-board canonical case-table
rows.notes: Per RO-G4: 6-state atomic. Cases: (i) ∞ → F (real, dim, inv); (ii) beyond C → between F-C (real, dim, inv); (iii) at C → at C (real, same-size, inv); (iv) between C-F → beyond C (real, mag, inv); (v) at F → ∞; (vi) between P-F → virtual, behind mirror, erect, mag. Plus +1 convex case (A9). The CBSE-board canonical case-table

rows.concept: For any real object: image is virtual, erect, diminished, behind the mirror. Single visual scene. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "convex mirror can form a real image with a real object" — fails dimensional check
rows.notes: For any real object: image is virtual, erect, diminished, behind the mirror. Single visual scene. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "convex mirror can form a real image with a real object" — fails dimensional check

rows.concept: Indian-context anchor: every Bajaj/TVS scooter, every auto-rickshaw rear-view. "Objects in mirror are closer than they appear" (the diminished image makes them seem far)
rows.notes: Indian-context anchor: every Bajaj/TVS scooter, every auto-rickshaw rear-view. "Objects in mirror are closer than they appear" (the diminished image makes them seem far)

rows.concept: Convex dome mirrors at supermarket corners + Metro station ceilings + bank ATM vestibules. Wide field of view at cost of magnification
rows.notes: Convex dome mirrors at supermarket corners + Metro station ceilings + bank ATM vestibules. Wide field of view at cost of magnification

rows.concept: If object moves with velocity v⃗ relative to mirror, image moves with v⃗ reflected across the mirror plane (parallel component preserved, perpendicular component reversed). Bridge to T6 kinematics. JEE-tested. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "image moves at same speed as object" — only true for perpendicular motion
rows.notes: If object moves with velocity v⃗ relative to mirror, image moves with v⃗ reflected across the mirror plane (parallel component preserved, perpendicular component reversed). Bridge to T6 kinematics. JEE-tested. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "image moves at same speed as object" — only true for perpendicular motion

rows.concept: Per RO-G5: own atomic. Marginal rays focus closer to mirror than paraxial rays; produces caustic curve + circle of least confusion. Fix: parabolic mirror (all parallel rays focus at one point). Indian anchors: automobile headlight reflector (Bajaj/TVS), solar cooker (MNRE SOLBOX program), satellite dish parabola
rows.notes: Per RO-G5: own atomic. Marginal rays focus closer to mirror than paraxial rays; produces caustic curve + circle of least confusion. Fix: parabolic mirror (all parallel rays focus at one point). Indian anchors: automobile headlight reflector (Bajaj/TVS), solar cooker (MNRE SOLBOX program), satellite dish parabola

rows.concept: Geometric proof: parabola defined as locus equidistant from focus + directrix ⇒ all parallel rays parallel to axis converge at focus exactly
rows.notes: Geometric proof: parabola defined as locus equidistant from focus + directrix ⇒ all parallel rays parallel to axis converge at focus exactly

rows.concept: Cassegrain telescope: parabolic primary + hyperbolic secondary. Indian anchor: 2.34m reflecting telescope at Kavalur, IIA Bangalore (NCERT §9.9.3 §"Cassegrain")
rows.notes: Cassegrain telescope: parabolic primary + hyperbolic secondary. Indian anchor: 2.34m reflecting telescope at Kavalur, IIA Bangalore (NCERT §9.9.3 §"Cassegrain")

rows.concept: Per RO-G6: own atomic. v_I_longitudinal = m² v_O; v_I_perpendicular = m v_O. JEE-Adv staple. Derived by differentiating mirror equation. NCERT Example 9.4 (jogger in side mirror) is the entry-level case
rows.notes: Per RO-G6: own atomic. v_I_longitudinal = m² v_O; v_I_perpendicular = m v_O. JEE-Adv staple. Derived by differentiating mirror equation. NCERT Example 9.4 (jogger in side mirror) is the entry-level case

rows.concept: d/dt(1/v + 1/u = 1/f) ⇒ -1/v² dv/dt - 1/u² du/dt = 0 ⇒ dv/dt = -(v²/u²) du/dt = -m² (du/dt)
rows.notes: d/dt(1/v + 1/u = 1/f) ⇒ -1/v² dv/dt - 1/u² du/dt = 0 ⇒ dv/dt = -(v²/u²) du/dt = -m² (du/dt)

rows.concept: All mirror formulas assume rays close to principal axis and small angles (sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ θ). The justification atomic for f = R/2 and 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. When violated → A11 spherical aberration. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "mirror formula works for any aperture" — fails for large-aperture beams
rows.notes: All mirror formulas assume rays close to principal axis and small angles (sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ θ). The justification atomic for f = R/2 and 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. When violated → A11 spherical aberration. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "mirror formula works for any aperture" — fails for large-aperture beams

rows.concept: μ = c/v; frequency unchanged across interface; wavelength changes (λ_medium = λ_vacuum/μ). Chain rule μ₁₂ × μ₂₃ × μ₃₁ = 1. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "denser optical medium = denser mass" — water > air optically but kerosene < water mass-density yet kerosene > water optically
rows.notes: μ = c/v; frequency unchanged across interface; wavelength changes (λ_medium = λ_vacuum/μ). Chain rule μ₁₂ × μ₂₃ × μ₃₁ = 1. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "denser optical medium = denser mass" — water > air optically but kerosene < water mass-density yet kerosene > water optically

rows.concept: f doesn't change at boundary (consequence of boundary continuity). Only λ and v change
rows.notes: f doesn't change at boundary (consequence of boundary continuity). Only λ and v change

rows.concept: Water 1.33, crown glass 1.52, dense flint 1.62, diamond 2.42 (NCERT Table 9.1). Just the values
rows.notes: Water 1.33, crown glass 1.52, dense flint 1.62, diamond 2.42 (NCERT Table 9.1). Just the values

rows.concept: μ_AB × μ_BC × μ_CA = 1. Useful for multi-medium problems
rows.notes: μ_AB × μ_BC × μ_CA = 1. Useful for multi-medium problems

rows.concept: μ₁ sin i = μ₂ sin r. Denser → rarer: ray bends away from normal. Rarer → denser: bends toward normal. Per RF-G1: own atomic. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "ray bends because it 'wants to' minimize path" — actually Fermat's-principle minimization of TIME, not path (the drowning-child analogy in NCERT §9.3 box)
rows.notes: μ₁ sin i = μ₂ sin r. Denser → rarer: ray bends away from normal. Rarer → denser: bends toward normal. Per RF-G1: own atomic. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "ray bends because it 'wants to' minimize path" — actually Fermat's-principle minimization of TIME, not path (the drowning-child analogy in NCERT §9.3 box)

rows.concept: Toward normal (rarer→denser) vs away from normal (denser→rarer). The "memorize-one-case" trick
rows.notes: Toward normal (rarer→denser) vs away from normal (denser→rarer). The "memorize-one-case" trick

rows.concept: If both boundaries parallel and same μ on both sides: emergent ray is parallel to incident. Internal angles cancel
rows.notes: If both boundaries parallel and same μ on both sides: emergent ray is parallel to incident. Internal angles cancel

rows.concept: μ = real_depth / apparent_depth (for normal-incidence viewing from above). Object appears raised. Multi-medium: total apparent shift = Σ (1 - 1/μ_i) × t_i. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "image is at same depth as object" — fishermen learn this empirically
rows.notes: μ = real_depth / apparent_depth (for normal-incidence viewing from above). Object appears raised. Multi-medium: total apparent shift = Σ (1 - 1/μ_i) × t_i. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "image is at same depth as object" — fishermen learn this empirically

rows.concept: The μ = real/apparent formula assumes small-angle (normal viewing). Oblique viewing gives more complex shift
rows.notes: The μ = real/apparent formula assumes small-angle (normal viewing). Oblique viewing gives more complex shift

rows.concept: h_app = h_1/μ_1 + h_2/μ_2. Indian-context: kerosene-on-water layered demos
rows.notes: h_app = h_1/μ_1 + h_2/μ_2. Indian-context: kerosene-on-water layered demos

rows.concept: A spear aimed at where a fish APPEARS misses — must aim BELOW. Indian-coastal fisherfolk anecdote anchor
rows.notes: A spear aimed at where a fish APPEARS misses — must aim BELOW. Indian-coastal fisherfolk anecdote anchor

rows.concept: For a parallel slab of thickness t and μ: lateral_shift = t × (1 - 1/μ) (normal-shift formula via DCP §31.4). Per RF-G1: own atomic. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "ray inside slab moves the image" — the displacement is purely a side-shift, image is at same depth-line
rows.notes: For a parallel slab of thickness t and μ: lateral_shift = t × (1 - 1/μ) (normal-shift formula via DCP §31.4). Per RF-G1: own atomic. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "ray inside slab moves the image" — the displacement is purely a side-shift, image is at same depth-line

rows.concept: If a slab sits between object and mirror, object appears closer by (1 - 1/μ)t. DCP Example 31.11
rows.notes: If a slab sits between object and mirror, object appears closer by (1 - 1/μ)t. DCP Example 31.11

rows.concept: sin i_c = 1/μ (going from denser μ to rarer 1). For i > i_c, total internal reflection — NO refracted ray. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "some light always transmits" — at TIR, ZERO transmits (idealized). Indian classroom demo: laser pointer in turbid water from below
rows.notes: sin i_c = 1/μ (going from denser μ to rarer 1). For i > i_c, total internal reflection — NO refracted ray. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "some light always transmits" — at TIR, ZERO transmits (idealized). Indian classroom demo: laser pointer in turbid water from below

rows.concept: Water 48.75°, crown glass 41.14°, dense flint 37.31°, diamond 24.41°. The "smaller critical angle = more brilliant" insight
rows.notes: Water 48.75°, crown glass 41.14°, dense flint 37.31°, diamond 24.41°. The "smaller critical angle = more brilliant" insight

rows.concept: TIR cannot happen rarer→denser. Common misconception correction
rows.notes: TIR cannot happen rarer→denser. Common misconception correction

rows.concept: Core + cladding (n_core > n_cladding); successive TIRs at every wall bounce; >95% transmission over 1km (NCERT). Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian anchors: BSNL/Reliance Jio fibre rollout in tier-2 cities; medical endoscope (gastroscope) for stomach exam (HCV §18.7)

rows.concept: n_core > n_cladding so light incident at core-cladding boundary exceeds critical angle. Numerical aperture concept
rows.notes: n_core > n_cladding so light incident at core-cladding boundary exceeds critical angle. Numerical aperture concept

rows.concept: NCERT: "about 2000 telephone signals" multiplexed per fibre. Indian-telecom rollout anchor
rows.notes: NCERT: "about 2000 telephone signals" multiplexed per fibre. Indian-telecom rollout anchor

rows.concept: Hot road heats air near surface; air layers near ground have lower μ (less dense, hotter); successive refraction bends light upward; at high enough incidence angle → TIR → observer sees inverted "puddle of sky" near road. Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian-context: every May/June highway between Delhi-Jaipur, Mumbai-Pune
rows.notes: Hot road heats air near surface; air layers near ground have lower μ (less dense, hotter); successive refraction bends light upward; at high enough incidence angle → TIR → observer sees inverted "puddle of sky" near road. Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian-context: every May/June highway between Delhi-Jaipur, Mumbai-Pune

rows.concept: Inverse case: cold air below, warm above (in seas near poles). Ships appear "floating in sky." Not Indian but worth a callout.
rows.notes: Inverse case: cold air below, warm above (in seas near poles). Ships appear "floating in sky." Not Indian but worth a callout.

rows.concept: 45°-45°-90° prism with light entering perpendicular to short side: TIR off the hypotenuse, exits perpendicular to other short side. Used to bend light by 90° or 180°, OR to invert an image without flipping (NCERT Fig.9.15 a,b,c). Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian-context: Indian Army periscope, classroom binoculars, naval submarine periscope
rows.notes: 45°-45°-90° prism with light entering perpendicular to short side: TIR off the hypotenuse, exits perpendicular to other short side. Used to bend light by 90° or 180°, OR to invert an image without flipping (NCERT Fig.9.15 a,b,c). Per RF-G3: own atomic.
